Goal: reduce attack frequency and severity, improve quality of life.

choices.text: Daily antihistamines and avoidance of triggers
choices.feedback: Antihistamines do not prevent HAE attacks because the mechanism is bradykinin, not histamine.
Corticosteroids are NOT effective for HAE prophylaxis. Long-term steroids would add side effects without benefit.

explanation: Long-term prophylaxis options for frequent HAE attacks include: (1) Lanadelumab (Takhzyro) — a monoclonal antibody targeting plasma kallikrein, given subcutaneously every 2-4 weeks, reduces attack frequency by >70%. (2) Subcutaneous C1-INH (Haegarda) — regular replacement therapy given twice weekly. (3) Berotralstat (Orladeyo) — an oral plasma kallikrein inhibitor taken daily. Older options include attenuated androgens (danazol) which upregulate C1-INH hepatic synthesis but carry significant androgenic side effects. IV C1-INH (Cinryze) given every 3-4 days is another option.

stem: Which medication class is CONTRAINDICATED in patients with HAE?

choices.feedback: PPIs are safe in HAE and may be used for GI symptoms. ACE inhibitors are the critical contraindication.
Statins are not contraindicated in HAE. ACE inhibitors are the key drug class to avoid.
Beta-blockers are not specifically contraindicated in HAE, though they should be used cautiously with epinephrine for anaphylaxis management.

Correct! ACE inhibitors inhibit angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE/kininase II), which is responsible for bradykinin degradation. In HAE patients (already bradykinin excess): ACE inhibitors → further impaired bradykinin clearance → can trigger severe, prolonged angioedema attacks. Also: estrogen-containing contraceptives can worsen HAE (estrogen increases Factor XII and kallikrein activity). Use progestin-only contraception instead.

explanation: ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in HAE because they block the degradation of bradykinin — the exact mediator that is already overproduced in HAE due to C1-inhibitor deficiency. ACE inhibitor use can precipitate severe angioedema attacks and worsen disease severity. This applies to all ACE inhibitors (enalapril, lisinopril, ramipril, etc.). ARBs (losartan, valsartan) are acceptable alternatives for hypertension management in HAE patients, as they do not affect bradykinin metabolism.

stem: What is the primary benefit of using a spacer/valved holding chamber (VHC) with a metered-dose inhaler (MDI)?

choices.text: It increases the total dose delivered from the canister
choices.feedback: The canister delivers the same dose regardless. The spacer affects how much reaches the lungs vs deposits in the oropharynx.

choices.text: It converts the medication to a finer mist than a nebulizer
choices.feedback: Spacers do not change particle generation — the MDI creates the aerosol. Spacers allow large particles to settle out, improving the fine-particle fraction that reaches the lungs.

Correct! Spacer/VHC benefits: (1) Holds aerosolized medication in suspension → allows slow inhalation even without perfect coordination with actuation. (2) Reduces particle velocity → less impaction on oropharynx (reduces thrush risk with ICS, reduces local side effects). (3) Filters out large particles → delivers more respirable particles (<5 μm) to lower airways. (4) Lung deposition increases from ~10-15% (MDI alone) to ~20-40% with spacer. Critical for: children, elderly, patients with poor coordination, ANY patient on ICS.

choices.text: It sterilizes the medication before inhalation
choices.feedback: Spacers do not sterilize anything. Their role is aerosol particle management and coordination assistance.

explanation: A spacer/valved holding chamber (VHC) reduces oropharyngeal deposition of inhaled medication by slowing aerosol velocity and allowing larger particles to settle in the spacer rather than impacting the back of the throat. This improves lung deposition from approximately 10-15% to 20-25% of the delivered dose. A spacer does not increase the total dose from the canister — it improves the fraction reaching the lungs. Spacers do not produce finer mist than nebulizers. Spacers do not sterilize medication. The clinical benefit is most important for inhaled corticosteroids, where reduced oropharyngeal deposition decreases the risk of oral candidiasis and dysphonia.

stem: What is the correct technique for using an MDI with a spacer?

choices.text: Shake MDI → attach to spacer → exhale normally → actuate once → slow deep inhalation → hold breath 10 seconds
choices.feedback: Correct! MDI + spacer technique: (1) Shake MDI 5 seconds, (2) Remove cap, attach to spacer, (3) Exhale normally (away from spacer), (4) Place mouthpiece in mouth with seal, (5) Actuate ONE puff into spacer, (6) Slow deep inhalation over 3-5 seconds (or multiple tidal breaths for young children using mask), (7) Hold breath 10 seconds, (8) Wait 30-60 seconds between puffs. Common errors: multiple actuations before inhaling (particles aggregate and settle), breathing too fast, not holding breath.

choices.text: Hold the spacer upside down while inhaling for better delivery
choices.feedback: Spacers should be held horizontally or upright. Inverting them can cause medication to pool and valve malfunction.

choices.text: Actuate 4 puffs into spacer at once, then breathe normally for 1 minute
choices.feedback: Multiple actuations into the spacer before inhaling reduces delivery — particles aggregate and settle on spacer walls. One puff at a time.

choices.text: Exhale into the spacer, actuate MDI, and take rapid deep breath
choices.feedback: Never exhale into the spacer (blows medication out). And rapid deep breathing reduces lung deposition.

explanation: Correct MDI + spacer technique: (1) Shake MDI well, (2) attach MDI to spacer, (3) exhale normally (away from spacer), (4) place mouthpiece in mouth and seal lips, (5) actuate ONE puff into spacer, (6) inhale slowly and deeply over 3-5 seconds, (7) hold breath for approximately 10 seconds, (8) exhale normally. Each puff should be actuated separately — multiple puffs into the spacer at once reduces delivery efficiency as particles settle and interact. Holding the spacer upside down would impair particle delivery. Rapid deep breaths increase oropharyngeal impaction, defeating the spacer's purpose.

stem: What side effect of inhaled corticosteroids is specifically reduced by spacer use and mouth rinsing?

choices.feedback: Adrenal suppression is a systemic effect from absorbed ICS. Spacer use primarily reduces local effects.
Growth suppression is a systemic effect. While spacer use may reduce total systemic absorption slightly, its primary benefit is reducing local oropharyngeal deposition.

Correct! ICS deposition on oral/pharyngeal mucosa → local immunosuppression → Candida overgrowth → thrush. Prevention: (1) Spacer/VHC reduces oropharyngeal deposition by 50-90%. (2) Rinse mouth (gargle and spit) after EVERY ICS dose. (3) Use lowest effective ICS dose. Other local ICS effects reduced by spacer: dysphonia (hoarseness from laryngeal myopathy), cough, throat irritation. If thrush develops: nystatin swish-and-swallow or clotrimazole troches.
Osteoporosis is a systemic effect of high-dose ICS over prolonged periods. Spacer use primarily addresses local oropharyngeal effects.

explanation: Oropharyngeal candidiasis (oral thrush) is the ICS side effect specifically reduced by spacer use and mouth rinsing. ICS particles deposited in the oropharynx create a local immunosuppressive environment favorable for Candida overgrowth. Spacers reduce oropharyngeal deposition, and rinsing/spitting removes residual drug. Adrenal suppression and osteoporosis are systemic effects related to overall absorbed dose, not local oropharyngeal deposition. Growth suppression in children is a systemic concern related to total ICS dose and duration, not amenable to spacer intervention.

stem: At what age can most children transition from MDI + spacer with mask to MDI + spacer with mouthpiece?

choices.feedback: Most 2-year-olds cannot reliably form a seal around a mouthpiece or coordinate inhalation. Mask remains appropriate.
Most children can use a mouthpiece by age 4-5. Waiting until 12 is unnecessary.
6-month-olds cannot form a seal around a mouthpiece or coordinate inhalation. They require a mask.

choices.text: Around age 4-5, when they can form a seal and coordinate slow inhalation
choices.feedback: Correct! Age-based device selection: 0-3 years: MDI + spacer with tight-fitting MASK (tidal breathing — multiple breaths through spacer). 4-5+ years: MDI + spacer with MOUTHPIECE (can coordinate slow deep breath + breath hold). 6+ years: may use DPI (dry powder inhaler) if adequate inspiratory flow. Mask fit is critical: air leaks reduce delivery dramatically. Crying during administration reduces lung deposition (mouth breathing preferred over nasal). Nebulizers are alternatives for any age but less portable and more time-consuming.

explanation: Most children can transition from MDI + spacer with face mask to MDI + spacer with mouthpiece around age 4-5, when they can reliably form a lip seal around the mouthpiece and coordinate slow, steady inhalation. Before this age, face masks are necessary because young children breathe through both nose and mouth and cannot maintain a mouthpiece seal. Age 2 is too young for mouthpiece coordination. Age 12 is unnecessarily late — most children master mouthpiece technique well before this. Six months is far too young for any mouthpiece use.

stem: A patient reports their rescue inhaler is not working as well. What is the most common reason for MDI treatment failure?

choices.feedback: Correct! Studies show 70-90% of patients use MDIs incorrectly. Most common errors: failure to shake canister (suspension medications settle), poor hand-breath coordination (actuating too early/late), breathing too fast (increases oropharyngeal deposition, reduces lung delivery), not holding breath (reduces small-particle deposition), not waiting between puffs, expired medication. Assessment: have patient demonstrate technique at EVERY visit. Even experienced patients develop technique drift. Adding a spacer corrects most coordination errors.
While cold weather can reduce MDI output slightly, technique is overwhelmingly the most common cause of perceived treatment failure.
Bronchodilator resistance is extremely rare. The most common issue is technique-related.

choices.text: Drug interaction with other inhaled medications
choices.feedback: Drug interactions between inhaled medications are rare. Poor technique is the dominant cause of MDI treatment failure.

explanation: Poor inhaler technique is the most common reason for MDI treatment failure, affecting up to 70-80% of patients. Common errors include poor actuation-inhalation coordination, inhaling too fast, not shaking the MDI, actuating multiple puffs simultaneously, and failing to hold breath after inhalation. Before escalating therapy, clinicians should always assess and correct inhaler technique. Canister malfunction from cold weather is rare. Medication resistance does not develop with inhaled bronchodilators or corticosteroids. Drug interactions between inhaled medications are not a recognized cause of treatment failure.

stem: Which asthma phenotype is most likely to respond to anti-IL-5 biologic therapy?

choices.feedback: Isolated EIB is typically managed with pre-exercise SABA or daily ICS and does not require biologic therapy.

choices.text: Severe eosinophilic asthma with persistently elevated blood/sputum eosinophils
choices.feedback: Correct! Eosinophilic asthma: elevated blood eosinophils (≥150-300 cells/μL) and/or sputum eosinophils (≥2%). IL-5 is the key cytokine driving eosinophil maturation, survival, and activation. Anti-IL-5 biologics: mepolizumab, reslizumab (anti-IL-5), benralizumab (anti-IL-5Rα — also depletes eosinophils via ADCC). Reduce exacerbations by 50%, allow oral steroid reduction, improve lung function in eosinophilic phenotype.
Obesity-related asthma is often non-eosinophilic (neutrophilic or pauci-granulocytic) and responds poorly to biologics targeting type 2 inflammation.
Allergic/IgE-driven asthma responds best to anti-IgE therapy (omalizumab). Anti-IL-5 targets a different pathway.

explanation: Anti-IL-5 biologics (mepolizumab, reslizumab) and the anti-IL-5 receptor antibody benralizumab specifically target the eosinophilic pathway. They are indicated for severe eosinophilic asthma with persistently elevated blood eosinophils (typically ≥150-300 cells/μL depending on the agent). Exercise-induced bronchoconstriction is usually managed with short-acting beta-agonists or ICS. Obesity-related asthma is typically T2-low and does not respond to eosinophil-targeting biologics. Allergic asthma with high IgE is better targeted by omalizumab (anti-IgE).

stem: What biomarker helps identify patients with Type 2 (T2-high) asthma who are candidates for biologic therapy?

choices.text: Blood eosinophils, FeNO (fractional exhaled nitric oxide), and serum IgE
choices.feedback: Correct! T2-high asthma biomarkers: Blood eosinophils ≥150-300/μL → eosinophilic phenotype → anti-IL-5 (mepolizumab, benralizumab), anti-IL-4Rα (dupilumab). FeNO ≥25 ppb → airway eosinophilic inflammation, IL-13 driven → responds to ICS, dupilumab, tezepelumab. Total IgE elevated + specific allergen sensitization → allergic phenotype → omalizumab (anti-IgE). T2-low asthma (low eosinophils, low FeNO, low IgE): fewer biologic options, may respond to tezepelumab (anti-TSLP), azithromycin, or bronchial thermoplasty.
Spirometry measures lung function severity but does not identify the inflammatory phenotype needed for biologic selection.
Procalcitonin is a marker for bacterial infection, not asthma phenotyping.
CRP and ESR are nonspecific inflammatory markers not useful for asthma phenotyping.

explanation: T2-high asthma is characterized by eosinophilic airway inflammation driven by IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13. The three key biomarkers that identify T2-high patients are blood eosinophils (≥150-300 cells/μL), FeNO (≥20-25 ppb indicating eosinophilic airway inflammation and iNOS upregulation), and serum total IgE (relevant for omalizumab eligibility, 30-700 IU/mL). Spirometry alone measures airflow obstruction but does not characterize the inflammatory phenotype. Procalcitonin is a bacterial infection marker, and CRP/ESR are nonspecific inflammatory markers not used for asthma phenotyping.

stem: What biologic therapy targets the broadest range of asthma phenotypes, including both T2-high and some T2-low patients?

choices.feedback: Correct! Tezepelumab targets thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), an epithelial-derived alarmin that sits UPSTREAM of the entire type 2 inflammatory cascade. By blocking TSLP: reduces eosinophils, IgE, FeNO, and IL-5/IL-13 simultaneously. NAVIGATOR trial: reduced exacerbations regardless of baseline eosinophil count — the ONLY biologic effective across T2-high AND some T2-low patients. Indicated for severe asthma ages ≥12 regardless of phenotype. Represents a paradigm shift in asthma biologics.
Mepolizumab is effective only in eosinophilic asthma (T2-high, eosinophil-dominant). It does not benefit T2-low patients.
Omalizumab only targets allergic (IgE-mediated) asthma — a subset of T2-high.
Dupilumab blocks IL-4 and IL-13 signaling — effective in eosinophilic and allergic asthma (T2-high) but not proven in T2-low asthma.

explanation: Tezepelumab (anti-TSLP) targets thymic stromal lymphopoietin, an epithelial alarmin released upstream of the entire T2 inflammatory cascade. By blocking TSLP, it reduces eosinophils, FeNO, and IgE levels. It is the only biologic indicated for severe asthma without phenotype or biomarker limitations, meaning it can benefit both T2-high and some T2-low patients. Mepolizumab and benralizumab target only the IL-5/eosinophil pathway. Omalizumab targets only IgE-mediated allergic inflammation. Dupilumab blocks IL-4/IL-13 but is still T2-pathway specific.
citation: Asthma. StatPearls. NBK430901. [Open Access] | Tezepelumab: anti-TSLP antibody, indicated for severe asthma without phenotype limitations. PMC9316404. [Open Access]

stem: A patient with allergic asthma has a total IgE of 450 IU/mL, positive skin tests to multiple aeroallergens, and persistent symptoms despite high-dose ICS/LABA. Which biologic is most appropriate?

choices.feedback: Tezepelumab is approved for severe asthma broadly, but omalizumab specifically targets the IgE pathway most relevant to this patient's allergic phenotype.
Mepolizumab targets eosinophilic inflammation. While this patient may have eosinophilia, the allergic phenotype (high IgE, positive SPT) makes omalizumab the most targeted choice.
Benralizumab targets the IL-5 receptor and is best for eosinophilic phenotype regardless of allergic status.

Correct! Omalizumab: monoclonal antibody that binds free IgE → prevents IgE binding to FcεRI on mast cells/basophils → reduces allergic cascade. Indications: moderate-severe allergic asthma (positive SPT/sIgE + total IgE 30-1500 IU/mL) ages ≥6. Dosing based on weight and IgE level (q2 or q4 weeks SC). Also reduces anaphylaxis risk (increases threshold) — now also approved for food allergy (reduces accidental reaction severity). Monitor for anaphylaxis risk (carry epinephrine after injections).

explanation: Omalizumab (anti-IgE) is the first-line biologic for severe allergic asthma with documented perennial allergen sensitization and IgE levels between 30-700 IU/mL (this patient has IgE 450 IU/mL with positive skin tests). Dosing is weight- and IgE-based, given subcutaneously every 2-4 weeks. Tezepelumab targets TSLP and is phenotype-agnostic but would not be the most targeted choice when the allergic phenotype is clearly established. Mepolizumab and benralizumab target IL-5/eosinophilic pathways and are preferred when eosinophilia is the dominant feature rather than IgE-mediated allergy.

stem: How does FeNO (fractional exhaled nitric oxide) help guide asthma management?

choices.text: FeNO diagnoses asthma versus COPD definitively
choices.feedback: While high FeNO suggests eosinophilic inflammation (more common in asthma), it is not diagnostic. Some COPD patients have high FeNO (asthma-COPD overlap).

choices.text: FeNO ≥25 ppb indicates eosinophilic/T2 airway inflammation
choices.feedback: Correct! FeNO reflects IL-13-driven inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) expression in airway epithelium → surrogate for eosinophilic/T2 inflammation. FeNO ≥25 ppb (adults): suggests T2-high → likely to respond to ICS and anti-T2 biologics. FeNO <25 ppb: less likely to respond to ICS escalation → consider alternative diagnoses or T2-low phenotype. Serial FeNO monitoring: helps assess ICS adherence (non-adherence → rising FeNO), guides therapy adjustment, and predicts exacerbation risk. Caveats: elevated by viral URI, reduced by smoking and bronchoconstriction.
Airway obstruction is measured by spirometry (FEV1). FeNO measures airway inflammation type.

choices.text: FeNO is only useful at initial diagnosis, not for monitoring
choices.feedback: FeNO is valuable for both initial phenotyping AND longitudinal monitoring (adherence, treatment response, exacerbation risk).

explanation: FeNO ≥25 ppb indicates eosinophilic/T2 airway inflammation because eosinophilic inflammation upregulates inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) in respiratory epithelium, increasing exhaled NO. Elevated FeNO predicts corticosteroid responsiveness, helps identify biologic candidates, and can monitor adherence to ICS therapy. FeNO does not definitively distinguish asthma from COPD (overlap exists). It measures airway inflammation, not obstruction severity (that is spirometry's role). FeNO is useful for ongoing monitoring, not just initial diagnosis — serial measurements guide ICS dose adjustments and assess treatment response.

stem: What does a positive skin prick test (SPT) indicate?

choices.text: The patient is clinically allergic and must avoid the allergen
choices.feedback: A positive SPT indicates sensitization (presence of specific IgE) but NOT necessarily clinical allergy. Many sensitized individuals are tolerant.

choices.text: The patient will develop anaphylaxis on next exposure
choices.feedback: SPT wheal size does NOT reliably predict reaction severity. A small wheal does not mean mild reaction, and a large wheal does not guarantee anaphylaxis.
SPT detects IgE, not IgG. IgG to foods is a normal physiologic response to food exposure and is not clinically meaningful.

choices.text: The patient is SENSITIZED (has allergen-specific IgE) but clinical relevance must be confirmed by history
choices.feedback: Correct! SPT detects cutaneous mast cell-bound IgE. A wheal ≥3 mm greater than negative control = positive = sensitization. Sensitivity ~90%, specificity ~50%. Positive predictive value varies widely (50-100% depending on allergen and clinical context). MUST be correlated with clinical history. Sensitized ≠ allergic (many people have positive SPT to foods they tolerate). The clinical decision to avoid an allergen depends on concordance between testing and symptoms, not testing alone.

explanation: A positive SPT indicates the presence of allergen-specific IgE on cutaneous mast cells (sensitization), not necessarily clinical allergy. Sensitization rates exceed clinical allergy rates — up to 50% of individuals with positive SPTs to aeroallergens may be clinically tolerant. Clinical relevance requires correlation with symptoms upon natural allergen exposure. A positive SPT does not predict anaphylaxis on next exposure. SPT detects IgE-mediated (Type I) sensitization, not IgG-mediated responses.
citation: Allergy Immunotherapy. StatPearls. NBK535367. [Open Access] | Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

stem: When should serum specific IgE (ImmunoCAP) be preferred over skin prick testing?

choices.text: When the patient has dermatographism, extensive eczema, cannot stop antihistamines, or has high anaphylaxis risk
choices.feedback: Correct! Specific IgE blood testing preferred when SPT cannot be reliably performed: Dermatographism (false-positive SPT), extensive skin disease (no uninvolved skin for testing), cannot discontinue antihistamines (suppress SPT — cetirizine needs 3-10 day washout), high anaphylaxis risk (SPT can rarely trigger systemic reaction), recent anaphylaxis (may be false-negative due to mast cell depletion). Also useful for: component-resolved diagnostics (CRD), monitoring allergen immunotherapy response, and standardized quantitative results.

choices.text: As a first-line screening test for all patients
choices.feedback: SPT is generally preferred as first-line when available — it's faster, cheaper, and provides immediate results with equivalent or better sensitivity.

choices.text: When skin testing shows equivocal results and you need a definitive answer
choices.feedback: Specific IgE is no more definitive than SPT — both detect sensitization. A positive sIgE does not confirm clinical allergy any more than a positive SPT does.

choices.text: Only for food allergy, never for aeroallergens
choices.feedback: Specific IgE testing is valid for both food and aeroallergens. The choice between SPT and sIgE depends on patient factors, not allergen type.

explanation: Serum specific IgE testing (ImmunoCAP) is preferred over SPT when: (1) dermatographism causes false-positive SPTs, (2) extensive eczema/dermatitis limits available skin testing sites, (3) antihistamines cannot be discontinued (e.g., chronic urticaria), or (4) the patient has a history of severe anaphylaxis making SPT riskier. SPT remains the first-line test when feasible due to higher sensitivity, lower cost, and immediate results. Serum IgE is not more definitive than SPT for equivocal results — both measure sensitization. Serum IgE testing works for both food and aeroallergens.

stem: What is component-resolved diagnostics (CRD) and when is it useful?

choices.text: DNA testing for genetic predisposition to allergies
choices.feedback: CRD is an immunologic test measuring specific IgE antibodies, not a genetic test.

choices.text: Measuring IgG4 levels to different food components
choices.feedback: IgG4 food panel testing has no validated clinical utility. CRD specifically measures IgE to allergenic protein components.

choices.text: Measuring IgE to individual allergenic proteins within a source to improve diagnostic specificity
choices.feedback: Correct! CRD: measures IgE to individual allergenic components rather than whole extracts. Examples: Peanut: Ara h 2 IgE (storage protein) = high risk for systemic reactions; Ara h 8 IgE (PR-10 homolog) = likely oral allergy syndrome only (cross-reactive with birch pollen, mild). Milk: casein IgE = persistent allergy; whey proteins = may outgrow. Tree nuts: seed storage proteins = genuine allergy; PR-10 = cross-reactivity (often tolerated). CRD helps avoid unnecessary dietary restrictions and predicts likelihood of outgrowing allergy.

choices.text: Measuring total IgE levels by component fractions
choices.feedback: CRD does not fractionate total IgE. It measures IgE to SPECIFIC allergenic proteins within a source.

explanation: Component-resolved diagnostics (CRD) measures IgE to individual allergenic proteins (molecular components) within an allergen source, improving diagnostic specificity. For example, peanut CRD distinguishes sensitization to stable storage proteins (Ara h 2 — high anaphylaxis risk) from labile PR-10 proteins (Ara h 8 — oral allergy syndrome only). This helps predict reaction severity and guide management. CRD is not DNA/genetic testing. IgG4 measurement to food components is unvalidated and not recommended. Total IgE fractionation is not CRD.
citation: Component Resolved Diagnostics. JACI Practice Parameter. [Open Access]

stem: Which allergy testing methods have NO scientific validity and should NOT be used?

choices.text: Skin prick testing and serum specific IgE
choices.feedback: Both SPT and sIgE are validated, evidence-based diagnostic tools. They are the cornerstone of allergy diagnosis.

choices.text: IgG food panels, applied kinesiology, cytotoxic testing, and electrodermal testing
choices.feedback: Correct! Unvalidated allergy tests: IgG/IgG4 food panels (IgG to food = normal immune response to food exposure, not pathologic), applied kinesiology (muscle testing while holding allergen), cytotoxic/leukocyte activation tests, electrodermal testing (Vega test), hair analysis, provocation-neutralization. These lead to unnecessary dietary restrictions, malnutrition, anxiety, and delayed appropriate care. Major allergy societies (AAAAI, EAACI, ACAAI) recommend AGAINST these tests.
Oral food challenges (especially DBPCFC) are the GOLD STANDARD for food allergy diagnosis.

choices.text: Patch testing and basophil activation testing
choices.feedback: Patch testing is validated for contact dermatitis (Type IV hypersensitivity). Basophil activation testing is an emerging validated research/clinical tool.

explanation: IgG food panels, applied kinesiology, cytotoxic testing (ALCAT), and electrodermal testing (Vega testing) have no scientific validity for diagnosing allergies and are explicitly not recommended by AAAAI, ACAAI, and EAACI. IgG antibodies to foods represent normal immune exposure, not allergy. These unvalidated tests lead to unnecessary dietary restrictions and delayed appropriate diagnosis. SPT and serum specific IgE are validated first-line allergy tests. Oral food challenges are the gold standard for food allergy diagnosis. Patch testing (for contact dermatitis) and basophil activation testing are scientifically validated methods.
citation: AAAAI Position Statement on Unproven Diagnostic Tests. [Open Access]

stem: A patient's SPT to cat is 8 mm wheal but they report living with cats without symptoms. What should you advise?

choices.text: They are sensitized but clinically tolerant
choices.feedback: Correct! This illustrates the key principle: sensitization ≠ clinical allergy. A positive SPT means IgE antibodies are present, but without concordant symptoms, the patient is clinically tolerant. No intervention needed for asymptomatic sensitization. Important: if the patient develops nasal/ocular/pulmonary symptoms upon increased exposure, the sensitization becomes clinically relevant. Allergen testing should always be interpreted in the context of clinical history — NEVER used as a sole basis for allergen avoidance or dietary restriction.

choices.text: Immediate cat removal — the SPT is positive so they're allergic
choices.feedback: A positive SPT alone does not mandate avoidance. Clinical correlation is essential.

choices.text: Repeat testing in 6 months — the result might have been false positive
choices.feedback: The result is a true positive for sensitization. Repeating the test will likely give the same result. The issue is interpretation, not test validity.
Immunotherapy is indicated for clinically relevant allergy causing symptoms, not for asymptomatic sensitization.

explanation: A positive SPT with no clinical symptoms represents sensitization without clinical allergy (clinically tolerant). Up to 50% of individuals sensitized to cat allergen (Fel d 1) on SPT tolerate cat exposure without symptoms. Management should be guided by clinical history, not SPT results alone. Immediate cat removal based solely on SPT positivity is inappropriate in an asymptomatic patient. The result is likely a true positive (sensitization) rather than false positive, so repeating adds no value. Prophylactic immunotherapy for an asymptomatic sensitized patient is not indicated — AIT requires both sensitization and correlating symptoms.

stem: A 35-year-old woman develops diffuse urticaria, throat tightness, and hypotension within minutes of a bee sting. What is the correct first-line treatment dose and route?

choices.text: IM epinephrine in the anterolateral thigh
choices.feedback: Correct! IM injection in the anterolateral thigh (vastus lateralis) provides the fastest and most reliable absorption. The thigh has better blood flow than the deltoid, especially during shock.
IV epinephrine is reserved for cardiac arrest or refractory anaphylaxis with hemodynamic monitoring. In the acute setting without IV access or monitoring, IM is safer and first-line.
Nebulized epinephrine treats upper airway edema (stridor) but does not provide systemic effects needed for anaphylaxis (hypotension, bronchospasm).
Subcutaneous absorption is slower and less reliable than IM, especially in shock when peripheral vasoconstriction limits subcutaneous blood flow. Deltoid is also inferior to thigh.

explanation: For anaphylaxis (urticaria + throat tightness + hypotension), the correct first-line treatment is IM epinephrine (0.3-0.5 mg of 1:1000/1 mg/mL in adults) injected in the anterolateral thigh. The thigh provides rapid absorption due to high vascularity. IM is preferred over IV push (which risks cardiac arrhythmias and is reserved for refractory cases with continuous monitoring). Nebulized epinephrine treats upper airway edema but does not address systemic hypotension. Subcutaneous injection in the deltoid has slower, less reliable absorption than IM thigh injection.
citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

stem: An emergency medicine resident reviews anaphylaxis fatality data. Which factor is most consistently associated with fatal anaphylaxis outcomes?

choices.text: Failure to administer IV corticosteroids within 30 minutes

explanation: Delayed or absent epinephrine administration is the most consistent risk factor for fatal anaphylaxis across fatality registries worldwide. Studies show that most anaphylaxis deaths occur in patients who either did not receive epinephrine or received it too late after symptom onset. Prompt IM epinephrine is the single most important intervention to prevent progression to cardiovascular collapse and death. Wrong concentration and IV corticosteroid timing are secondary concerns. Fluid resuscitation supports hemodynamics but cannot substitute for epinephrine's receptor-mediated effects.
citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

stem: A 22-year-old with known peanut allergy develops throat tightness and diffuse urticaria after accidental exposure. She has her EpiPen but wants to wait and see if symptoms improve. What is the correct counsel?

choices.text: Administer the EpiPen immediately — throat tightness with urticaria after a known allergen meets anaphylaxis criteria
Give oral diphenhydramine first and use epinephrine only if she develops respiratory distress
Drive to the emergency department before using the EpiPen so medical supervision is available
Administer the EpiPen only if she develops hypotension or loses consciousness

explanation: Throat tightness with diffuse urticaria after known allergen exposure meets diagnostic criteria for anaphylaxis (involvement of skin/mucosal tissue PLUS respiratory compromise). Immediate epinephrine administration is critical — delay is the primary risk factor for fatal outcomes. Waiting to 'see if symptoms improve' allows progression to airway obstruction, cardiovascular collapse, or both. Oral diphenhydramine alone is insufficient for anaphylaxis. Driving to an ED before administering epinephrine wastes critical time. Waiting for hypotension or loss of consciousness means waiting for cardiovascular collapse — epinephrine should be given at the first signs of anaphylaxis.
citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

stem: A medical student asks why epinephrine is first-line for anaphylaxis rather than antihistamines or corticosteroids. Which explanation best describes epinephrine's multi-receptor mechanism?

choices.text: Alpha-1 vasoconstriction reverses hypotension and edema; beta-1 increases cardiac output; beta-2 causes bronchodilation and stabilizes mast cells
Direct antagonism of histamine H1 and H2 receptors combined with leukotriene inhibition
Stimulation of adrenal cortisol release producing rapid anti-inflammatory effects
Primarily beta-2 bronchodilation with minimal cardiovascular effects at IM doses

explanation: Epinephrine is first-line for anaphylaxis because of its unique multi-receptor mechanism: alpha-1 vasoconstriction (reverses hypotension, reduces angioedema), beta-1 positive inotropy/chronotropy (increases cardiac output), and beta-2 bronchodilation plus mast cell/basophil stabilization (reduces further mediator release via cAMP elevation). No other single drug addresses all three pathophysiologic components of anaphylaxis simultaneously. Antihistamines only block one mediator. Corticosteroids have delayed onset (hours). Beta-2 effects alone would not address hypotension.
citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

stem: When prescribing epinephrine auto-injectors at discharge after an anaphylaxis episode, how many devices should be prescribed and why?

choices.text: Two devices — approximately 10-20% of episodes require a second dose, and biphasic reactions may occur
One device — a single IM dose resolves virtually all anaphylaxis episodes
Two devices — the second is intended for a family member or bystander to use simultaneously
Three devices — one for home, one for work, and one for travel

explanation: Two epinephrine auto-injectors are prescribed at discharge because 10-20% of anaphylaxis episodes require a repeat dose, and biphasic reactions may recur hours after initial resolution. Patients and caregivers should be trained on proper auto-injector use, understand that the second device is for repeat dosing of the same patient if symptoms recur or persist, and carry both devices at all times. A single device is insufficient. The rationale is repeat dosing need, not simultaneous use by a bystander. Location-specific distribution is practical but not the primary prescribing justification.
citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

stem: What is the mechanism by which allergen immunotherapy (AIT) achieves clinical tolerance?

choices.feedback: IgE may decrease over time but is not eliminated. The mechanism involves immune modulation, not IgE elimination.

choices.text: Induction of allergen-specific IgA in the nasal mucosa
choices.feedback: While mucosal immunity changes occur, the primary mechanism is Treg induction and IgG4 blocking antibodies, not IgA.

choices.text: Desensitization of mast cell histamine receptors
choices.feedback: AIT does not desensitize histamine receptors. It modulates the upstream immune response to the allergen.

choices.text: Immune deviation from Th2 to Th1/Treg responses with increased blocking IgG4 antibodies
choices.feedback: Correct! AIT mechanisms: (1) Induction of allergen-specific T regulatory cells (Tregs) → produce IL-10, TGF-β → suppresses Th2 inflammation. (2) Immune deviation: shift from Th2 (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13) toward Th1 (IFN-γ) profile. (3) Increased allergen-specific IgG4 (blocking antibody) → competes with IgE for allergen binding → prevents mast cell cross-linking. (4) Decreased mast cell/basophil reactivity over time. (5) Long-term: decreased allergen-specific IgE. Unique among allergy treatments: AIT is disease-modifying (not just symptom control).

explanation: Allergen immunotherapy (AIT) works by shifting the immune response from a Th2-dominant pattern to a Th1/T-regulatory cell (Treg) pattern. Tregs produce IL-10 and TGF-β, which suppress eosinophils, mast cells, and basophils while promoting antibody class switching from IgE to IgG4 blocking antibodies. IgG4 competes with IgE for allergen binding, preventing mast cell/basophil cross-linking and degranulation. AIT does not eliminate allergen-specific IgE — IgE initially rises then gradually declines over years. IgA plays a role in SLIT but is not the primary mechanism. Mast cell histamine receptors are not directly desensitized by AIT.

stem: What are the two routes of allergen immunotherapy administration?

choices.feedback: Oral immunotherapy exists for food allergy, but conventional aeroallergen immunotherapy uses different routes.
IV and IM routes are not used for allergen immunotherapy due to high systemic reaction risk.

Correct! SCIT: weekly injections during build-up (3-6 months) → monthly maintenance × 3-5 years. Must be administered in medical office with 30-minute observation (anaphylaxis risk ~0.1% per injection). SLIT: daily sublingual tablet/drops (self-administered at home after first dose). Available tablets: Grastek (grass), Ragwitek (ragweed), Odactra (dust mite). SLIT: safer (local reactions common, systemic rare), more convenient. SCIT: more allergens can be combined, stronger evidence for polysensitized patients.

choices.text: Topical skin patches and nasal sprays
choices.feedback: Epicutaneous immunotherapy (EPIT) for food allergy is investigational. Nasal spray immunotherapy is not standard practice.

explanation: The two established routes of allergen immunotherapy are subcutaneous (SCIT) and sublingual (SLIT). SCIT involves escalating-dose injections given in a medical office with 30-minute observation periods, reaching a maintenance dose given every 2-4 weeks. SLIT uses allergen tablets or drops placed under the tongue daily; the first dose is given under medical supervision, then subsequent doses are self-administered at home. Oral immunotherapy (OIT) exists for food allergies but is distinct from traditional AIT. IV and IM routes are not used. Epicutaneous immunotherapy (EPIT) via skin patches is investigational.

stem: What is the most serious adverse effect of subcutaneous immunotherapy (SCIT)?

choices.text: Anaphylaxis — risk ~0.1% per injection, most reactions within 30 minutes
choices.feedback: Correct! SCIT carries a small but real risk of systemic anaphylaxis. Risk factors: uncontrolled asthma (strongest predictor), dosing errors, injections during peak allergen season, high sensitivity. Safety protocols: 30-minute in-office observation after every injection, epinephrine immediately available, assess for asthma control before each injection, dose reduction if large local reactions. Fatalities: ~1 per 2.5 million injections. This is why SCIT requires medical supervision (unlike SLIT which can be home-administered after first dose).
AIT does not cause immunosuppression. It modulates the immune response specifically to the treated allergens.
Local reactions are common but not serious. The most concerning adverse effect is systemic.

choices.text: Development of new allergies from the injected extracts
choices.feedback: AIT does not cause new allergic sensitizations. Evidence suggests it may PREVENT development of new sensitizations and reduce asthma risk.

explanation: Anaphylaxis is the most serious adverse effect of SCIT, occurring at a rate of approximately 0.2% per injection. Most systemic reactions occur within 30 minutes of injection, which is why a 30-minute in-office observation period is mandatory. Fatal anaphylaxis is extremely rare but has been reported. Risk factors include uncontrolled asthma, dosing errors, and concurrent beta-blocker use (which impairs epinephrine response). SCIT does not cause chronic immunosuppression — it modulates specific immune pathways. Injection site reactions (local swelling, redness) are common but not the most serious. AIT does not create new allergies from injected extracts.

stem: How long must allergen immunotherapy be continued to achieve long-lasting benefit after discontinuation?

choices.text: Lifelong — benefits stop immediately upon discontinuation
choices.feedback: Unlike pharmacotherapy, AIT's disease-modifying effects persist after discontinuation — this is its key advantage.

choices.text: 3-5 years of maintenance therapy for sustained disease-modifying effect
choices.feedback: Correct! AIT: minimum 3 years, typically 3-5 years of maintenance for durable disease modification. Benefits persist for years AFTER discontinuation (unlike all other allergy medications which work only while being taken). SCIT: build-up (weekly × 3-6 months) → maintenance (monthly × 3-5 years). SLIT: daily × 3-5 years. Evidence: effects persist ≥3 years after stopping 3-year course. Disease modification: reduces symptom severity, medication use, prevents new sensitizations, and may prevent allergic rhinitis → asthma progression in children. This makes AIT unique among allergy treatments.
6 weeks is far too short. Immune modulation requires months to years of consistent allergen exposure to achieve durable tolerance.
3 months is only the build-up phase for SCIT. This is insufficient for lasting disease modification.

explanation: Clinical guidelines recommend 3-5 years of maintenance allergen immunotherapy to achieve sustained disease-modifying benefits that persist after discontinuation. Studies show that 3 years of SCIT or SLIT provides continued clinical benefit for at least 2-3 years after stopping therapy. AIT also has preventive effects — it can reduce development of new allergen sensitizations and prevent progression from allergic rhinitis to asthma (demonstrated primarily in children). Benefits do not require lifelong therapy. Six weeks or 3 months is insufficient to achieve disease modification — the build-up phase alone typically takes 3-6 months.

stem: Which patients are the BEST candidates for allergen immunotherapy?

choices.text: Patients with non-allergic rhinitis and negative allergy tests
choices.feedback: AIT requires IgE-mediated sensitization. Non-allergic rhinitis does not respond to AIT because there is no IgE-mediated component to modulate.

choices.text: All patients with any positive allergy test
choices.feedback: AIT requires clinical correlation — a positive test alone without concordant symptoms is not an indication.
AIT is approved for ages 5+ for SCIT (some SLIT products for different ages). It is effective in both children and adults.

choices.text: Patients with IgE-mediated allergic rhinitis/asthma with confirmed sensitization to clinically relevant allergens, whose symptoms are inadequately controlled by avoidance and medications
choices.feedback: Correct! Ideal AIT candidates: (1) IgE-mediated disease (allergic rhinitis, allergic asthma, venom allergy) confirmed by SPT/sIgE, (2) Clinical symptoms correlating with sensitized allergens, (3) Inadequate control with avoidance + pharmacotherapy OR desire to reduce medication burden, (4) Limited number of clinically relevant allergens (polysensitization less predictable). Contraindications: uncontrolled/severe asthma (risk of fatal anaphylaxis), beta-blocker use (makes anaphylaxis harder to treat), immunosuppressed (less effective). Venom immunotherapy is especially effective: reduces sting anaphylaxis from 60% to <5%.

explanation: Ideal AIT candidates have confirmed IgE-mediated disease (positive skin prick test or serum specific IgE) with clinical symptoms correlating to the identified allergens, AND inadequate control despite allergen avoidance measures and pharmacotherapy. AIT is indicated for allergic rhinitis, allergic asthma, and Hymenoptera venom anaphylaxis. Non-allergic rhinitis patients (negative allergy tests) have no IgE-mediated component to target. Not all patients with positive allergy tests need AIT — only those with clinically relevant sensitization and inadequate symptom control. AIT can be used in children but is not limited to those under 5.

stem: What is the primary indication for immunoglobulin replacement therapy (IVIG/SCIG)?

choices.feedback: While high-dose IVIG is used for some autoimmune conditions, the PRIMARY indication is immunodeficiency.
IV iron is used for iron deficiency. IVIG provides IgG antibodies for immune deficiency.
IVIG/SCIG provides immunoglobulin G antibodies, not vitamins.

choices.text: Primary antibody deficiency disorders to prevent recurrent infections
choices.feedback: Correct! Immunoglobulin replacement: provides passive IgG antibodies to patients who cannot produce sufficient antibodies. Primary indications: Primary humoral immunodeficiencies (CVID, X-linked agammaglobulinemia, specific antibody deficiency), secondary hypogammaglobulinemia (CLL, multiple myeloma, post-rituximab). Goal: maintain IgG trough levels ≥500-800 mg/dL to reduce infection frequency. Routes: IVIG (intravenous, q3-4 weeks, hospital/infusion center) or SCIG (subcutaneous, weekly or biweekly, self-administered at home). SCIG: more stable IgG levels, fewer systemic reactions, improved quality of life.

explanation: The primary indication for immunoglobulin replacement therapy is primary antibody deficiency disorders (e.g., common variable immunodeficiency, X-linked agammaglobulinemia, specific antibody deficiency) to prevent recurrent sinopulmonary and systemic bacterial infections. These patients cannot produce adequate protective antibodies. IVIG/SCIG provides passive IgG antibodies. Secondary indications include IVIG's immunomodulatory effects in autoimmune conditions (ITP, Kawasaki disease, Guillain-Barré) at higher doses, but replacement for primary immunodeficiency remains the core indication.

stem: What is the key advantage of subcutaneous immunoglobulin (SCIG) over intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG)?

choices.text: SCIG can be given intramuscularly as well
choices.feedback: SCIG is specifically subcutaneous. Intramuscular immunoglobulin (IMIG) exists but is rarely used for replacement therapy due to volume limitations and pain.

choices.text: Self-administration at home with fewer systemic adverse reactions
choices.feedback: Correct! SCIG advantages: (1) Home self-administration → improved quality of life, fewer hospital visits. (2) More stable IgG levels (no peak-trough variation of monthly IVIG). (3) Fewer systemic reactions (headache, chills, anaphylaxis are rare vs IVIG). (4) No IV access needed (important for children, patients with poor veins). Disadvantages: local site reactions (swelling, redness — common but usually mild), more frequent administration (weekly vs monthly), limited per-site volume (may need multiple sites). Training essential for proper technique.
SCIG may have modest cost advantages (no infusion center fees) but the primary advantages are clinical and quality-of-life related.

choices.text: SCIG achieves higher peak IgG levels
choices.feedback: SCIG actually produces LOWER peak levels but more stable steady-state levels (less peak-trough variation). This is an advantage, not a limitation.

explanation: SCIG's key advantage over IVIG is the ability to self-administer at home, with fewer systemic adverse reactions. SCIG provides steadier-state IgG levels (avoiding the peak-trough fluctuations of monthly IVIG), which may improve clinical outcomes and quality of life. SCIG has a much lower rate of systemic reactions (headache, fever, anaphylaxis) because absorption is gradual from subcutaneous tissue. Local injection site reactions (swelling, erythema) are common but mild. SCIG eliminates the need for IV access and infusion center visits.

stem: What serious adverse reaction can occur with IVIG infusion, especially in patients with IgA deficiency?

choices.feedback: IVIG-associated hemolysis can occur (from anti-A/anti-B isohemagglutinins in the product) but it is not specific to IgA deficiency and is not the most serious concern.
While aseptic meningitis can occur with IVIG, the IgA-specific concern is different.
IVIG does not cause immunosuppression. It provides supplementary IgG. High-dose IVIG for autoimmune conditions has immunomodulatory effects but does not cause permanent immunosuppression.

choices.text: Anaphylaxis from anti-IgA antibodies in IgA-deficient patients
choices.feedback: Correct! Patients with complete IgA deficiency can develop IgG anti-IgA antibodies → anaphylaxis when exposed to IgA in standard IVIG products (which contain trace IgA). Prevention: screen for IgA deficiency and anti-IgA antibodies before IVIG. If at risk: use IgA-depleted products (Gammagard S/D) or switch to SCIG (lower systemic exposure, fewer reactions). Other IVIG adverse effects: infusion reactions (headache, chills, fever — rate-dependent), aseptic meningitis, thrombotic events (especially in elderly, hypercoagulable states), renal failure (sucrose-containing products).

explanation: IgA-deficient patients (~1:500 prevalence) may develop IgG or IgE anti-IgA antibodies. When these patients receive IVIG (which contains trace IgA), anaphylaxis can occur from anti-IgA-mediated immune complex formation or IgE-mediated mast cell activation. This is the most feared serious adverse reaction of IVIG. Prevention: screen for IgA deficiency before starting IVIG, use IgA-depleted products (e.g., Gammagard S/D) for IgA-deficient patients, and consider SCIG as an alternative (lower IgA content, slower absorption reduces systemic reaction risk).

stem: How do you monitor the effectiveness of immunoglobulin replacement therapy?

choices.feedback: CBC monitoring is not the primary measure of Ig replacement efficacy. IgG trough levels and infection rates are the key parameters.

choices.text: Skin prick testing to common allergens
choices.feedback: Skin testing is for allergy diagnosis, not for monitoring immunoglobulin replacement effectiveness.

Correct! Monitoring: (1) IgG trough level: measured just before next IVIG dose (or any time for SCIG — steady-state). Target varies by patient but generally ≥500-800 mg/dL; some patients require higher levels (individualize based on clinical response). (2) Clinical endpoints: reduced frequency of sinopulmonary infections, fewer antibiotic courses, fewer hospitalizations, improved quality of life. (3) Monitor for end-organ damage: pulmonary function tests (bronchiectasis prevention), CT chest if needed. Dose adjustments based on both trough levels AND clinical response.
IgE is not a relevant monitoring parameter for Ig replacement. IgG trough levels and infection frequency are used.

explanation: Immunoglobulin replacement therapy effectiveness is monitored by IgG trough levels measured just before the next infusion (for IVIG) or at steady state (for SCIG). The goal is typically ≥500-800 mg/dL, with many experts targeting trough levels that minimize breakthrough infections in the individual patient. Clinical response (reduction in infection frequency and severity) is equally important. Some patients require higher trough levels for adequate protection. Trough levels should be measured every 3-6 months and after dose adjustments.

stem: In which clinical scenario is serum specific IgE (sIgE) testing preferred over skin prick testing?

choices.text: When the patient has severe eczema, dermatographism, or cannot stop antihistamines
choices.feedback: Correct! sIgE (ImmunoCAP) preferred when: extensive dermatitis/eczema (insufficient normal skin for SPT), dermatographism (false-positive SPT), unable to discontinue antihistamines (interfere with SPT), history of severe anaphylaxis (very small risk of systemic reaction from SPT), uncooperative patient (young children). Advantages: unaffected by medications or skin conditions, quantitative results, no anaphylaxis risk. Limitations: lower sensitivity than SPT, more expensive, delayed results.

choices.text: When testing for contact dermatitis to metals or fragrances
choices.feedback: Contact dermatitis is Type IV (cell-mediated) and diagnosed by PATCH testing, not SPT or sIgE.

choices.text: When the patient has normal skin and is not taking any medications
choices.feedback: In this ideal scenario, SPT is preferred (faster, cheaper, more sensitive). sIgE is preferred when SPT is impractical.

choices.text: When the clinician suspects a non-IgE-mediated allergy like FPIES
choices.feedback: sIgE tests are NEGATIVE in non-IgE-mediated conditions like FPIES. Neither SPT nor sIgE can diagnose non-IgE food allergies.

explanation: Serum specific IgE (sIgE) testing is preferred when SPT cannot be performed reliably: severe eczema (insufficient normal skin), dermatographism (false-positive wheals from mechanical pressure), or inability to stop antihistamines (which suppress SPT). Contact dermatitis is Type IV (cell-mediated) hypersensitivity diagnosed by patch testing, not sIgE. Normal skin with no interfering medications favors SPT as first-line. FPIES is a non-IgE-mediated food allergy — sIgE would be negative and unhelpful.

stem: What is the intradermal skin test primarily used for in allergy practice?

choices.feedback: Latex allergy can be tested by SPT (where available) or sIgE. Intradermal testing is used more broadly for drugs and venoms when SPT is inconclusive.
Intradermal testing is NOT recommended for food allergy — too high false-positive rate and increased systemic reaction risk.

choices.text: Screening for all environmental allergies before starting immunotherapy
choices.feedback: SPT is the standard for environmental allergy testing before immunotherapy. Intradermal testing is used selectively, not as first-line screening.

choices.text: Drug allergy testing and venom allergy evaluation when SPT is negative but clinical suspicion remains
choices.feedback: Correct! Intradermal testing (injection of dilute allergen into dermis): higher sensitivity than SPT (detects lower levels of IgE) but lower specificity (more false positives). Primary uses: penicillin allergy evaluation (standardized protocol: SPT first → if negative, intradermal), Hymenoptera venom allergy (SPT negative with convincing sting reaction history), and some drug allergies. NOT recommended for food testing (unacceptable false-positive rate, increased anaphylaxis risk).

explanation: Intradermal skin testing (IDT) is primarily used for drug allergy evaluation (especially penicillin) and venom allergy testing when SPT is negative but clinical suspicion remains high. IDT is more sensitive but less specific than SPT — it can detect lower levels of IgE sensitization. For Hymenoptera venom allergy, IDT at concentrations up to 1 μg/mL is performed when SPT is negative in patients with convincing sting anaphylaxis history. IDT is not used exclusively for latex. It is not first-line for food allergy (high false-positive rate and risk of systemic reactions). It is not a routine screening tool for environmental allergies.

stem: A patient with seasonal rhinoconjunctivitis has a positive SPT to birch pollen. They also report oral tingling when eating raw apples, cherries, and celery. What explains this?

choices.text: They have developed new IgE-mediated food allergies to multiple fruits
choices.feedback: This pattern is not independent food allergy but pollen-food cross-reactivity.

choices.text: Oral allergy syndrome (pollen-food allergy syndrome) due to cross-reactive PR-10 proteins
choices.feedback: Correct! OAS/PFAS: IgE directed against birch Bet v 1 (PR-10 protein family) cross-reacts with homologous proteins in raw fruits/vegetables/nuts: apple (Mal d 1), cherry (Pru av 1), celery (Api g 1), carrot, hazelnut, soybean. Symptoms: isolated oropharyngeal pruritus/tingling with RAW food (proteins denatured by cooking → cooked forms tolerated). Rarely progresses to systemic anaphylaxis (<2%). Diagnosis: clinical history + CRD showing Bet v 1 and cross-reactive component IgE. Management: avoid raw trigger foods, or cook/microwave them. Birch immunotherapy may reduce OAS symptoms.

choices.text: Histamine intolerance from a diamine oxidase deficiency
choices.feedback: Histamine intolerance causes systemic symptoms (headache, flushing, GI) from histamine-rich foods, not the isolated oral symptoms triggered by specific raw plant foods described here.

choices.text: Non-allergic food intolerance from enzyme deficiency
choices.feedback: The reproducible pattern with specific raw fruits that cross-react with birch pollen is classic OAS, an IgE-mediated cross-reactivity phenomenon.

explanation: Oral allergy syndrome (OAS/pollen-food allergy syndrome) occurs due to cross-reactive PR-10 proteins shared between birch pollen (Bet v 1) and fruits/vegetables (apple Mal d 1, cherry Pru av 1, peach Pru p 1, carrot Dau c 1). Patients experience oral/pharyngeal tingling, itching, and mild angioedema when eating raw cross-reactive fruits. These are heat-labile proteins, so cooked forms are typically tolerated. This is not new primary food allergy — the sensitization is driven by birch pollen IgE cross-reacting with homologous food proteins. Histamine intolerance presents with broader systemic symptoms unrelated to specific pollen seasons.
citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

stem: What distinguishes Type 2 (T2-high) asthma from non-Type 2 asthma?

choices.text: T2-high asthma only occurs in children
choices.feedback: T2-high asthma occurs in both children and adults. It is actually the predominant phenotype across all ages.

choices.text: Non-T2 asthma is always caused by occupational exposures
choices.feedback: Non-T2 asthma has many causes (obesity-related, neutrophilic, smoking-related) and is not limited to occupational triggers.

choices.text: T2-high asthma is always mild and well-controlled
choices.feedback: T2-high asthma can range from mild to severe. Many severe asthma patients have T2-high phenotypes.

choices.text: T2-high features eosinophilic inflammation, elevated FeNO, and response to biologics targeting IL-4/IL-5/IL-13
choices.feedback: Correct! T2-high asthma: driven by Th2 cytokines (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13) → eosinophilic airway inflammation, elevated FeNO (>25 ppb), elevated serum IgE, blood eosinophils >150-300. Responds to: ICS (backbone), anti-IgE (omalizumab), anti-IL-5/IL-5R (mepolizumab, benralizumab), anti-IL-4Rα (dupilumab), anti-TSLP (tezepelumab). Non-T2 (T2-low): neutrophilic or paucigranulocytic, lower FeNO, poorer ICS response. Biomarker-guided phenotyping determines biologic eligibility.

explanation: T2-high asthma is driven by Th2 lymphocytes and ILC2 cells producing IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13, resulting in eosinophilic inflammation, elevated FeNO, elevated IgE, and responsiveness to biologics targeting these pathways. Non-T2 (T2-low) asthma features neutrophilic or paucigranulocytic inflammation with normal eosinophils and FeNO, and responds poorly to corticosteroids. T2-high asthma occurs in both children and adults. Non-T2 asthma has multiple causes beyond occupational exposures (obesity, smoking, infections). T2-high asthma ranges from mild to severe and is not always well-controlled — severe T2-high asthma is the primary indication for biologic therapy.
citation: Asthma. StatPearls. NBK430901. [Open Access] | PMC5765385. [Open Access]

stem: What biomarker is most useful for guiding ICS dose adjustments in asthma?

choices.feedback: PEF measures airflow obstruction (functional status) but does not reflect underlying inflammation type. FeNO more directly indicates eosinophilic inflammation and ICS responsiveness.

Correct! FeNO reflects eosinophilic airway inflammation driven by IL-13 → upregulates iNOS in airway epithelium → increased NO production. FeNO-guided treatment: high FeNO (>50 ppb adults) suggests ICS-responsive inflammation → increase ICS; low FeNO (<25 ppb) suggests non-eosinophilic inflammation or good control → consider ICS reduction. Studies show FeNO-guided management reduces exacerbations. Confounders: smoking ↓, atopy ↑, viral infection ↑, allergen exposure ↑, age, height.
Sputum neutrophils identify neutrophilic asthma (typically ICS-unresponsive). FeNO is the practical biomarker for ICS-responsive eosinophilic inflammation.
Total IgE helps determine omalizumab eligibility/dosing but is not used for ICS dose titration.

explanation: FeNO is the most useful biomarker for guiding ICS dose adjustments because it directly reflects eosinophilic airway inflammation responsive to corticosteroids. Elevated FeNO in a patient on ICS suggests poor adherence, inadequate dosing, or persistent T2 inflammation requiring step-up. Decreasing FeNO after ICS initiation confirms therapeutic response. PEF measures airflow obstruction but not the inflammatory phenotype driving it. Sputum neutrophil counts reflect neutrophilic inflammation (not ICS-responsive). Serum total IgE changes slowly and is primarily used for omalizumab dosing, not routine ICS adjustment.

stem: Which biologic targets IL-5 and is approved for severe eosinophilic asthma?

choices.feedback: Omalizumab targets IgE (anti-IgE), not IL-5. It is approved for moderate-severe allergic asthma.
Secukinumab targets IL-17A and is used for psoriasis and spondyloarthropathies, not asthma.

Correct! Mepolizumab (Nucala): anti-IL-5 monoclonal antibody → reduces eosinophil production and survival → decreases blood and sputum eosinophils. Approved for severe eosinophilic asthma (blood eos ≥150 or ≥300 depending on criteria). Also: benralizumab (anti-IL-5Rα — depletes eosinophils via ADCC, near-complete depletion), reslizumab (anti-IL-5, IV). Dupilumab targets IL-4Rα (blocks both IL-4 and IL-13). Tezepelumab (anti-TSLP) works upstream and is effective in both T2-high and some T2-low asthma.
Dupilumab targets IL-4 receptor alpha (blocks IL-4 and IL-13), not IL-5. It works on a different T2 pathway.

explanation: Mepolizumab is a monoclonal antibody that directly binds and neutralizes IL-5, the key cytokine driving eosinophil production, maturation, and survival. It is indicated for severe eosinophilic asthma (blood eosinophils ≥150 cells/μL) as add-on maintenance therapy. Omalizumab targets IgE, not IL-5. Secukinumab targets IL-17A (used for psoriasis/ankylosing spondylitis, not asthma). Dupilumab targets the IL-4 receptor α subunit, blocking both IL-4 and IL-13 signaling — it is approved for moderate-to-severe eosinophilic asthma but works via the IL-4/IL-13 pathway, not IL-5.

stem: A patient with severe asthma has normal FeNO, normal eosinophils, high BMI, and poor response to high-dose ICS. Which asthma phenotype is this?

choices.feedback: EIB is triggered by exercise and responds well to pre-exercise SABA. This patient's profile suggests a distinct non-T2 obesity phenotype.

Correct! Obesity-related asthma: often non-T2 phenotype with normal eosinophils and FeNO, predominantly adult-onset, female predominance. Mechanisms: mechanical (reduced lung volumes, airway closure), metabolic (systemic inflammation, insulin resistance, adipokines), and oxidative stress. Poor ICS response. Management priorities: weight loss (even 5-10% improves control), exercise, address GERD/OSA (common comorbidities). Biologics targeting T2 pathways generally less effective. Emerging: thermoplasty, anti-TSLP (tezepelumab shows some T2-low benefit).
Allergic eosinophilic asthma would have elevated FeNO and eosinophils with good ICS response. This is T2-low.
AERD features nasal polyps, aspirin sensitivity, and is typically eosinophilic (T2-high). This patient has T2-low features.

explanation: This presentation — severe asthma with normal FeNO, normal eosinophils, high BMI, and poor ICS response — is classic for obesity-related non-T2 (T2-low) asthma. Obesity contributes to asthma through mechanical restriction, systemic inflammation (adipokines, IL-6, TNF-α), and metabolic dysregulation rather than eosinophilic pathways. These patients respond poorly to ICS because their inflammation is not T2-driven. Weight loss is a key therapeutic intervention. Exercise-induced bronchospasm presents with symptoms only during/after exercise. Allergic eosinophilic asthma would show elevated eosinophils and FeNO. AERD (Samter triad) features asthma, nasal polyps, and NSAID sensitivity.
citation: Asthma. StatPearls. NBK430901. [Open Access] | PMC5765385. [Open Access]

stem: What defines aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD)?

choices.text: Drug-induced lupus from chronic aspirin use
choices.feedback: Drug-induced lupus is caused by hydralazine, procainamide, and isoniazid, not aspirin. AERD is a respiratory syndrome, not an autoimmune condition.

choices.text: Allergic asthma that worsens with any NSAID due to IgE-mediated drug allergy
choices.feedback: AERD is NOT IgE-mediated. It is caused by dysregulated arachidonic acid metabolism.

choices.text: Asthma with nasal polyps that only occurs in aspirin factory workers
choices.feedback: AERD is not an occupational disease — it can occur in anyone. It is an intrinsic dysregulation of prostaglandin/leukotriene metabolism.

choices.text: The triad of asthma, nasal polyps, and respiratory reactions to COX-1 inhibitors (aspirin/NSAIDs)
choices.feedback: Correct! AERD (Samter's triad): chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps + asthma + respiratory reactions to aspirin/NSAIDs (COX-1 inhibitors). Pathophysiology: COX-1 inhibition → arachidonic acid shunted to 5-LOX pathway → overproduction of cysteinyl leukotrienes (LTC4, LTD4, LTE4) → bronchoconstriction, nasal congestion, eosinophilic inflammation. Diagnosis: aspirin challenge (oral or nasal). Management: aspirin desensitization — reduces polyp recurrence and improves asthma; leukotriene modifiers; biologics (dupilumab effective for polyps + asthma). COX-2 selective agents (celecoxib) usually tolerated.

explanation: AERD (aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease), also known as Samter triad, is defined by three co-occurring conditions: (1) asthma, (2) chronic rhinosinusitis with recurrent nasal polyps, and (3) respiratory hypersensitivity reactions to COX-1 inhibitors (aspirin and NSAIDs). The mechanism is non-IgE-mediated — COX-1 inhibition shunts arachidonic acid metabolism toward the lipoxygenase pathway, increasing cysteinyl leukotrienes (LTC4, LTD4, LTE4) while reducing protective PGE2. Reactions typically occur within 30 minutes to 3 hours of NSAID ingestion. AERD affects approximately 7% of adult asthmatics. Leukotriene receptor antagonists (montelukast) and aspirin desensitization are key management strategies.
citation: Aspirin-Exacerbated Respiratory Disease. PMC3005316. [Open Access] | PMC5951071. [Open Access]

stem: What is the primary benefit of using a spacer (valved holding chamber) with a metered-dose inhaler (MDI)?

choices.feedback: Correct! Spacers provide multiple benefits: slow aerosol velocity → smaller particle size reaches lower airways → improved lung deposition (increases from ~10-15% without spacer to ~20-25% with spacer). Reduces oropharyngeal deposition → fewer local side effects (thrush, dysphonia from ICS). Eliminates need for precise hand-breath coordination → critical for children, elderly, and patients with poor technique. Reduces the fast-moving "cold Freon" effect that causes patients to stop inhaling.

choices.text: It converts the medication to a dry powder formulation
choices.feedback: A spacer works with MDIs (pressurized aerosol), not dry powder inhalers. DPIs have their own inhalation technique and do not use spacers.

choices.text: It increases the total dose delivered by the MDI
choices.feedback: A spacer does not increase the total dose. It improves delivery efficiency of the existing dose.

choices.text: It eliminates the need for rinsing the mouth after ICS use
choices.feedback: Spacers reduce but do not eliminate oropharyngeal deposition. Mouth rinsing after ICS is still recommended to prevent candidiasis and dysphonia.

explanation: A spacer reduces oropharyngeal deposition by slowing aerosol velocity and allowing large particles to settle before inhalation, improving the respirable fraction reaching the lower airways. It does not convert medication to dry powder formulation — that is a different device class (DPI). It does not increase the total dose delivered by the MDI — it improves where the dose is deposited. While spacers reduce oropharyngeal ICS deposition, mouth rinsing is still recommended as an additional protective measure and is not eliminated by spacer use alone.

stem: A patient using an ICS-MDI develops oral thrush. What is the most effective intervention?

choices.feedback: Oral corticosteroids would cause MORE immunosuppression and more candidiasis risk. The goal is to improve inhaler technique.
Stopping ICS risks uncontrolled asthma. The solution is improving drug delivery technique, not stopping treatment.
Systemic antifungals are unnecessary for ICS-related thrush. Fixing technique (spacer + rinsing) addresses the root cause.

choices.text: Add a spacer and ensure mouth rinsing after each ICS use
choices.feedback: Correct! Oral candidiasis (thrush) from ICS is caused by oropharyngeal steroid deposition → local immunosuppression → Candida overgrowth. Prevention: spacer use (reduces oropharyngeal deposition by 80%+), rinse mouth and spit after EVERY ICS dose, use lowest effective ICS dose. Treatment of active thrush: topical antifungal (nystatin swish-and-swallow or clotrimazole troches). If persistent, consider: switching to DPI (different deposition pattern) or prodrug ICS (ciclesonide — activated in lungs, minimal oropharyngeal activity).

explanation: For ICS-associated oral thrush, the most effective intervention combines adding a spacer (to reduce oropharyngeal drug deposition) and ensuring consistent mouth rinsing with water and spitting after each ICS use. These measures together address the root cause — local immunosuppression from oropharyngeal ICS deposition. Switching to oral corticosteroids would worsen immunosuppression systemically. Discontinuing all inhaled medications leaves asthma uncontrolled. Prophylactic oral fluconazole treats the symptom without addressing the cause and adds unnecessary drug burden.

stem: What is the correct technique for using a dry powder inhaler (DPI)?

choices.text: Slow, gentle inhalation with a 10-second breath hold
choices.feedback: Slow inhalation is for MDIs. DPIs require the opposite — fast and forceful inhalation.

choices.text: Quick, deep, forceful inhalation to generate sufficient inspiratory flow
choices.feedback: Correct! DPI technique: exhale away from device (moisture deactivates powder) → seal lips around mouthpiece → inhale quickly and deeply (peak inspiratory flow rate >target rate depending on device) → hold breath 5-10 seconds. DPIs are breath-actuated (no propellant) → the patient's inspiratory force disperses the powder into respirable particles. Key difference from MDI: MDI = slow steady inhalation; DPI = fast forceful inhalation. Patients with severe obstruction or children <5 may lack sufficient inspiratory force for DPIs → use MDI + spacer instead.

choices.text: Multiple shallow breaths in rapid succession
choices.feedback: Multiple shallow breaths do not generate the sustained inspiratory flow needed to disaggregate and disperse the dry powder formulation.

choices.text: Shake vigorously before each use, then breathe normally
choices.feedback: Shaking is for MDIs (to suspend propellant). DPIs should NOT be shaken. Breathing normally is insufficient — forceful inhalation is required.

explanation: Dry powder inhalers (DPIs) require a quick, deep, forceful inhalation to generate sufficient inspiratory flow (typically ≥30-60 L/min depending on device resistance) to deaggregate and aerosolize the powder formulation. This is the key difference from MDI technique, which requires slow, steady inhalation. Slow, gentle inhalation describes MDI technique and is incorrect for DPIs. Multiple shallow breaths do not generate adequate flow. DPIs should NOT be shaken before use — shaking can cause powder loss. DPIs are breath-actuated and do not require actuation-inhalation coordination.

stem: A 3-year-old child needs inhaled albuterol for acute wheezing. What is the preferred delivery device?

choices.feedback: Breath-actuated MDIs require coordinated inspiratory effort that a 3-year-old cannot reliably perform. Spacer + mask is needed.

choices.text: MDI with spacer and face mask
choices.feedback: Correct! For children <4-5 years: MDI + spacer with attached face mask (tight seal over nose and mouth). The child breathes tidally (normal breaths) through the mask for 5-6 breaths after actuation. Advantages over nebulizer: faster delivery, more portable, equivalent drug delivery, less crying/distress. For children >4-5 years: MDI + spacer with mouthpiece (transition from mask). Nebulizer is alternative when: child is in severe distress, unable to cooperate with MDI + spacer, or in emergency settings.
Children <5 typically cannot generate sufficient inspiratory flow for DPIs.
Without a spacer, MDI coordination is impossible for a 3-year-old, and oropharyngeal deposition would be excessive with minimal lung delivery.

explanation: For a 3-year-old with acute wheezing, the preferred delivery device is an MDI with spacer and face mask. This combination is at least as effective as nebulization for bronchodilator delivery in young children and offers advantages of portability, faster administration, and lower cost. Breath-actuated MDIs require coordinated inspiratory effort beyond a 3-year-old's ability. DPIs require forceful inhalation (≥30 L/min) that young children cannot generate reliably. Direct MDI without spacer results in excessive oropharyngeal deposition and minimal lung delivery in any patient, especially young children.

stem: What is the most common reason inhaled medications fail to control asthma?

choices.feedback: Airway remodeling does contribute to severe asthma over time, but it is far less common as a cause of poor control than technique and adherence issues.

Correct! Studies show 70-80% of patients use inhalers incorrectly, and ICS adherence rates are only ~50%. Incorrect technique → drug deposited in mouth/throat instead of lungs → subtherapeutic lung doses → poor control despite adequate prescriptions. At every visit: assess technique (observe patient using device), verify adherence (prescription refill records, electronic monitoring), and address barriers. Common errors: not shaking MDI, poor hand-breath coordination (MDI without spacer), insufficient inspiratory flow (DPI), not holding breath, exhaling into DPI.
True pharmacologic resistance to ICS is rare. The most common issue is patient-related.
Beta-blockers can worsen asthma but this is uncommon as a primary cause of medication failure. Poor technique is overwhelmingly the most common reason.

explanation: Poor inhaler technique and non-adherence are the most common reasons inhaled medications fail to control asthma, accounting for a majority of apparent treatment failures. Studies show up to 70-80% of patients demonstrate at least one critical inhaler technique error, and adherence to daily controller medications averages only 50%. Before diagnosing refractory asthma, clinicians must verify technique, adherence, and trigger avoidance. While airway remodeling occurs in chronic severe asthma, it is not the most common cause of treatment failure. Medication resistance does not develop to ICS or bronchodilators. Beta-blocker interaction, while important, is uncommon.

stem: What is the mechanism of allergen immunotherapy (AIT)?

choices.text: Desensitization of mast cells through repeated histamine exposure
choices.feedback: AIT does not directly desensitize mast cells through histamine. It works upstream through T-cell immune modulation and blocking antibody production.
AIT does not eliminate sIgE. Initially sIgE may even increase. The mechanism involves immune deviation and tolerance.

choices.text: Suppression of all immune responses including protective immunity
choices.feedback: AIT is allergen-specific — it modulates the response to the specific allergens administered without broadly suppressing immunity.

choices.text: Immune deviation from Th2 to Th1/Treg response with production of blocking IgG4 antibodies
choices.feedback: Correct! AIT mechanism: repeated allergen exposure → induces allergen-specific Tregs (producing IL-10, TGF-β) → immune deviation from Th2 to Th1 → decreased IL-4, IL-5, IL-13 → reduced eosinophilic inflammation. Also: increased allergen-specific IgG4 (blocking antibodies) that compete with IgE for allergen binding → prevent mast cell cross-linking. Long-term: decreased mast cell and basophil reactivity, mucosal IgA production. Disease-modifying: prevents new sensitizations and progression from rhinitis to asthma.

explanation: AIT induces immune deviation from a Th2-dominant response (driving IgE, eosinophilia, and allergic inflammation) to a Th1/Treg response. Tregs secrete IL-10, which promotes IgG4 blocking antibody production, suppresses allergen-specific IgE over time, and reduces mast cell, basophil, and eosinophil recruitment. IgG4 antibodies compete with IgE for allergen binding, preventing cross-linking of surface IgE on mast cells. AIT does not work by desensitizing mast cells through repeated histamine exposure. IgE is not completely eliminated. AIT is allergen-specific and does not suppress protective immunity broadly.

stem: What is the minimum recommended duration for allergen immunotherapy to achieve disease-modifying benefits?

choices.feedback: Standard recommendation is 3-5 years. Beyond 5 years provides diminishing additional benefit for most patients, though some may benefit from longer courses.

Correct! Standard AIT duration: 3-5 years for sustained disease modification. Benefits that persist after discontinuation: reduced symptoms, decreased medication use, prevention of new allergen sensitizations, prevention of asthma development in children with allergic rhinitis. Subcutaneous immunotherapy (SCIT): weekly buildup → monthly maintenance. Sublingual immunotherapy (SLIT): daily tablet or drops. Discontinuing before 3 years → higher relapse rates. Some patients (e.g., mastocytosis with venom allergy) require lifelong therapy.
One year of AIT provides symptomatic improvement but is insufficient for the sustained, disease-modifying immunologic changes.
