Six months is too short for disease modification. Symptom improvement begins around 3-6 months, but sustained benefit requires years of treatment.

explanation: A minimum of 3-5 years of maintenance immunotherapy is recommended for lasting disease-modifying benefits. This duration allows sufficient immune remodeling — Treg induction, IgG4 production, and gradual IgE decline — to persist after treatment discontinuation. Shorter durations (6 months, 1 year) may provide temporary symptom improvement but are insufficient for sustained tolerance. Ten years is longer than necessary for most patients, though some may choose to continue if they had relapse after initial discontinuation.

stem: Which of the following is a contraindication to initiating subcutaneous allergen immunotherapy?

choices.text: Concurrent allergic rhinitis and mild persistent asthma
choices.feedback: Mild-moderate allergic asthma with rhinitis is actually an ideal indication for AIT — it can prevent asthma progression.

Correct! Uncontrolled/severe asthma with FEV1 <70% is a contraindication because: higher risk of severe systemic reactions during injections, risk of fatal bronchospasm. Asthma must be stabilized (FEV1 >70%, well-controlled symptoms) before starting SCIT. Other contraindications: concurrent beta-blocker use (relative — complicates epinephrine treatment if reaction occurs), unstable cardiac disease, immunodeficiency (may not mount therapeutic response). NOT a contraindication: pregnancy (don't start, but can continue if already on maintenance).
While SCIT is used more cautiously in very young children, age <5 alone is not an absolute contraindication. SLIT tablets are approved for ages 5+.
Polysensitization is common and does not preclude AIT. Immunotherapy mixes can include multiple relevant allergens.

explanation: Severe uncontrolled asthma is a contraindication to SCIT because it significantly increases the risk of life-threatening anaphylaxis and severe bronchospasm during immunotherapy. Patients with active asthma exacerbations should not receive injections until asthma is stabilized. Concurrent allergic rhinitis and mild persistent asthma is actually a common and appropriate indication for AIT. Age under 5 is a relative consideration (not absolute contraindication) — AIT is used in young children when benefits outweigh risks. Multiple allergen sensitizations do not contraindicate AIT; multi-allergen immunotherapy is routinely prescribed.

stem: What advantage does sublingual immunotherapy (SLIT) have over subcutaneous immunotherapy (SCIT)?

choices.text: SLIT is more effective than SCIT for all allergens
choices.feedback: SCIT generally shows larger effect sizes than SLIT. The advantage of SLIT is safety and convenience, not superior efficacy.

choices.text: SLIT works in 2 weeks compared to 6 months for SCIT
choices.feedback: Both SLIT and SCIT require months to achieve clinical benefit. Neither works in 2 weeks.

choices.text: SLIT can be administered at home with significantly lower risk of systemic reactions
choices.feedback: Correct! SLIT advantages: home administration after first dose in clinic (no monthly office visits), very low anaphylaxis risk (~1 in 100 million doses vs ~1 in 1 million for SCIT), no needle injections, better adherence in some populations (children, needle-phobic). Available SLIT tablets: grass (Grastek), ragweed (Ragwitek), dust mite (Odactra), mixed grass (Oralair). SLIT limitations: limited allergen options compared to SCIT (can customize SCIT mixes), local oral side effects (sublingual pruritus/swelling), daily dosing required.

choices.text: SLIT completely replaces the need for pharmacotherapy
choices.feedback: Both SLIT and SCIT reduce but typically do not completely eliminate the need for symptom-relief medications.

explanation: SLIT's primary advantage is home administration after the first supervised dose, with a significantly lower risk of systemic reactions compared to SCIT. SLIT anaphylaxis is extremely rare (estimated 11 nonfatal cases per billion doses). The most common SLIT adverse effects are local oromucosal reactions (itching, swelling under the tongue). SLIT is not more effective than SCIT for all allergens — comparative data suggest similar or slightly lower efficacy for some allergens. SLIT does not work faster than SCIT. SLIT does not eliminate the need for pharmacotherapy — it is used as adjunctive disease-modifying therapy.

stem: What is the most important safety measure when administering subcutaneous immunotherapy injections?

choices.text: Administering injections only in hospital emergency departments
choices.feedback: SCIT is routinely administered in allergy outpatient offices with proper safety protocols. Emergency department setting is not required.

choices.text: Pre-treat with oral antihistamines 30 minutes before every injection
choices.feedback: Antihistamine pre-treatment is optional (may reduce local reactions) but is not the most critical safety measure.

choices.text: 30-minute in-office observation period after each injection with epinephrine immediately available
choices.feedback: Correct! Mandatory 30-minute observation: most systemic reactions (including anaphylaxis) occur within 30 minutes of SCIT injection. Requirements: trained staff to recognize anaphylaxis, epinephrine auto-injectors/vials immediately available, resuscitation equipment, written emergency protocol. Assess patient before each injection: check for current asthma symptoms, illness, recent reaction. Measure FEV1 or PEF if asthmatic. Reduce dose if: previous systemic reaction, new vial (potency variation), gap in schedule, uncontrolled asthma.

choices.text: Aspiration before injection to ensure the needle is not in a blood vessel
choices.feedback: While aspiration is commonly taught, the 30-minute observation period is the most critical safety measure, as systemic reactions require immediate recognition and treatment.

explanation: The 30-minute in-office observation period after each SCIT injection is the most critical safety measure because most anaphylactic reactions to SCIT occur within this window. Epinephrine and emergency resuscitation equipment must be immediately available, and personnel must be trained in anaphylaxis management. Injections are given in medical offices (not necessarily emergency departments) where a physician is present. Pretreatment with oral antihistamines is sometimes used but is not the most important safety measure and may mask early signs of systemic reactions. Aspiration before injection is a technique consideration but not the primary safety protocol.

stem: A 60-year-old man on metoprolol for heart failure develops anaphylaxis to a bee sting. His IM epinephrine does not adequately resolve hypotension. What is the pathophysiologic basis and recommended next agent?

choices.text: Beta-blockade may blunt epinephrine's beta-adrenergic effects; administer IV glucagon which increases cAMP independent of adrenergic receptors
Beta-blockers completely neutralize all epinephrine activity; switch to norepinephrine infusion
Beta-blockers cause epinephrine to produce unopposed alpha vasoconstriction; administer IV phentolamine
Beta-blockers prevent mast cell stabilization; administer IV cromolyn sodium

explanation: Beta-blockers (metoprolol) competitively antagonize beta-1 and beta-2 adrenergic receptors, blunting epinephrine's positive inotropic/chronotropic effects (beta-1) and bronchodilatory/mast cell stabilizing effects (beta-2). IV glucagon is the recommended next agent because it increases intracellular cAMP through glucagon receptors independent of the beta-adrenergic pathway, providing positive inotropic and chronotropic effects despite beta-blockade. Beta-blockers do not completely neutralize all epinephrine activity — alpha-1 vasoconstriction is preserved. Phentolamine (alpha-blocker) would worsen hypotension. IV cromolyn has no role in acute anaphylaxis management.
citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

stem: A nurse asks whether any conditions contraindicate administering epinephrine to a patient with suspected anaphylaxis. Which statement is most accurate?

choices.text: There are no absolute contraindications to IM epinephrine for life-threatening anaphylaxis
Pregnancy is an absolute contraindication due to uterine artery vasoconstriction
Sulfite allergy contraindicates EpiPen use since it contains sodium metabisulfite
Age over 65 with coronary artery disease is an absolute contraindication

explanation: There are no absolute contraindications to IM epinephrine for life-threatening anaphylaxis. The benefit of treating a potentially fatal condition always outweighs the risks. Pregnancy is not a contraindication — maternal death from untreated anaphylaxis is a far greater risk to mother and fetus than epinephrine's effects. While EpiPen contains sodium metabisulfite, sulfite sensitivity is a relative concern that does not contraindicate use in anaphylaxis. Elderly patients with coronary artery disease may have increased cardiovascular risk from epinephrine, but untreated anaphylaxis carries a greater mortality risk.
citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

stem: What advantage does subcutaneous immunoglobulin (SCIG) have over IVIG?

choices.text: SCIG achieves higher peak IgG levels
choices.feedback: SCIG actually produces LOWER peaks and HIGHER troughs (more stable levels) compared to IVIG's high peak/low trough pattern.

choices.text: SCIG is significantly cheaper than IVIG
choices.feedback: The immunoglobulin product cost is similar. SCIG saves on infusion center/nursing costs but the drug expense is comparable.

choices.text: Home self-administration, stable serum IgG levels, and fewer systemic side effects
choices.feedback: Correct! SCIG advantages: self-infusion at home (independence, no IV access, no hospital visits), more stable/physiologic IgG levels (avoids IVIG peak-trough cycling → patients feel better consistently), fewer systemic side effects (headache, fatigue less common than IVIG), no IV access-related risks. SCIG disadvantages: local infusion site reactions (swelling, redness — usually improve over months), more frequent dosing (weekly vs monthly), lower volume per site (multiple sites may be needed). Facilitated SCIG (Hylenex + Ig): allows larger volumes per site, monthly dosing.

choices.text: SCIG can treat autoimmune conditions while IVIG cannot
choices.feedback: Actually, IVIG is used for autoimmune/inflammatory conditions at high immunomodulatory doses. SCIG is primarily used for replacement therapy at lower doses.

explanation: SCIG advantages over IVIG include: home self-administration (eliminating infusion center visits), stable serum IgG levels (weekly/biweekly dosing avoids peak-trough fluctuations), and fewer systemic side effects (gradual subcutaneous absorption vs rapid IV infusion). SCIG allows greater patient autonomy and schedule flexibility. Local injection site reactions are the main side effect. SCIG does not require IV access, reducing infection and thrombotic risks associated with venous access.

stem: What is the most common serious adverse effect of IVIG infusion?

choices.text: Severe infusion reactions (headache, fever, chills) and, rarely, aseptic meningitis, thrombotic events, or hemolytic anemia
choices.feedback: Correct! IVIG adverse effects: Common: headache (most common), fever, chills, myalgia, fatigue, nausea — usually rate-related (slow infusion rate). Serious: aseptic meningitis (severe headache, nuchal rigidity — resolves in days), thromboembolic events (stroke, PE, DVT — risk increased with high dose, hypercoagulable states), hemolytic anemia (anti-A/anti-B isoagglutinins in product → DAT-positive hemolysis, especially in non-O blood type patients at high doses). Special: IgA-deficient patients with anti-IgA IgE → anaphylaxis risk (use IgA-depleted product).
True IgG-mediated anaphylaxis is rare. The most dangerous IVIG reactions occur in specific patient populations.
IVIG can cause osmotic nephropathy (especially sucrose-containing products) but this is not the most common effect. It's seen primarily in patients with pre-existing renal disease.
IVIG provides passive antibody protection; it does not cause immunosuppression. High-dose immunomodulatory IVIG may temporarily modulate immune responses in autoimmune conditions.

explanation: The most common serious adverse effects of IVIG include severe rate-related infusion reactions (headache, fever, chills, myalgias, nausea), and rare but serious complications: aseptic meningitis (severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia within 24-48 hours), thromboembolic events (stroke, DVT, PE, MI — especially in patients with cardiovascular risk factors), acute kidney injury (particularly with sucrose-containing formulations), and hemolytic anemia (from anti-A/anti-B antibodies in IVIG). Anaphylaxis in IgA-deficient patients is the most feared reaction.

stem: An IgA-deficient patient needs immunoglobulin replacement. What precaution is necessary?

choices.text: Give oral immunoglobulin instead of IV or SC
choices.feedback: Oral immunoglobulin is not absorbed systemically and is not a viable replacement therapy route.

choices.text: No special precautions needed — all IVIG products are safe
choices.feedback: IgA-deficient patients with anti-IgA antibodies can have severe anaphylactic reactions to standard IVIG containing trace IgA.

choices.text: Use IgA-depleted immunoglobulin product and pre-medicate; consider SCIG route
choices.feedback: Correct! Selective IgA deficiency (~1:500 prevalence) + anti-IgA IgE antibodies → risk of anaphylaxis from IgA in standard Ig products. Precautions: use IgA-depleted IVIG product (e.g., Gammagard S/D — very low IgA content), pre-medicate with antihistamines/corticosteroids, slow initial infusion rate with close monitoring, have epinephrine available. SCIG may be safer (slower absorption, less likely to trigger systemic reactions). Not all IgA-deficient patients have anti-IgA antibodies — test first. Most IgA-deficient patients don't need Ig replacement unless they also have IgG subclass deficiency.

choices.text: Administer standard IVIG but at half the normal dose
choices.feedback: Reducing the dose does not address the anaphylaxis risk from IgA content. An IgA-depleted product is needed.

explanation: For IgA-deficient patients requiring immunoglobulin replacement, precautions include: (1) use IgA-depleted IVIG products (e.g., Gammagard S/D, which has the lowest IgA content), (2) premedicate with antihistamines and corticosteroids, (3) consider SCIG as an alternative route (lower IgA content and slower absorption reduce anaphylaxis risk), (4) have epinephrine immediately available during infusions, and (5) start with slow infusion rates. Some IgA-deficient patients tolerate standard IVIG products if they lack anti-IgA antibodies.

stem: A patient with suspected penicillin allergy reports a childhood rash. What is the most appropriate initial evaluation?

choices.text: Penicillin skin testing followed by oral challenge if negative
choices.feedback: Correct! Penicillin skin testing (PST): puncture + intradermal with major determinant (penicilloyl-polylysine/Pre-Pen) and minor determinants. Negative predictive value >97%. If skin test negative → graded oral amoxicillin challenge to confirm tolerance. This approach safely de-labels >90% of patients and allows access to first-line beta-lactams. AUA/AAAAI guidelines strongly recommend allergy evaluation before labeling patients.
Serum IgE (ImmunoCAP) can be used but has lower sensitivity (~50%) than skin testing. Skin testing remains the gold standard for penicillin allergy evaluation.

choices.text: Direct oral amoxicillin challenge without any preliminary testing
choices.feedback: For patients with a history of severe reactions (anaphylaxis), skin testing should precede oral challenge. Direct challenges are acceptable only for very low-risk histories (GI side effects, remote mild rash).
Over 90% of patients labeled penicillin-allergic are NOT truly allergic when formally tested. Avoidance without evaluation leads to suboptimal antibiotic choices.

explanation: For suspected penicillin allergy with a childhood rash history, the standard evaluation is penicillin skin testing (major determinant penicilloyl-polylysine and minor determinant mixture, plus penicillin G) followed by graded oral amoxicillin challenge if skin testing is negative. Over 90% of patients labeled penicillin-allergic are found to be tolerant on formal evaluation. Serum-specific IgE alone has insufficient sensitivity. Direct oral challenge without preliminary skin testing is inappropriate in patients with a history consistent with possible IgE-mediated reaction. Permanent beta-lactam avoidance is unnecessarily restrictive — de-labeling allows access to first-line antibiotics.
citation: Drug Allergy. AAAAI/ACAAI Practice Parameter. [Open Access]

stem: What does a positive skin prick test (SPT) to an aeroallergen indicate?

choices.feedback: A positive SPT indicates sensitization (presence of allergen-specific IgE), NOT necessarily clinical allergy. Must correlate with symptoms.
SPT detects Type I (IgE-mediated) immediate hypersensitivity, NOT Type IV (T-cell mediated delayed). Patch testing is used for Type IV reactions.

choices.text: IgE-mediated sensitization that must be correlated with clinical history
choices.feedback: Correct! Positive SPT = wheal ≥3 mm larger than negative control = presence of allergen-specific IgE on mast cells. Sensitization ≠ clinical allergy. Many sensitized individuals are asymptomatic. Clinical relevance requires correlation with: timing of symptoms relative to exposure, symptom pattern, exposure history. Serum-specific IgE (sIgE) provides similar information quantitatively. Component-resolved diagnostics (CRD) can help distinguish true allergy from cross-reactivity.
SPT does not measure complement activation. It measures IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation (Type I hypersensitivity).

explanation: A positive SPT to an aeroallergen indicates IgE-mediated sensitization — allergen-specific IgE bound to cutaneous mast cells causes wheal-and-flare upon allergen cross-linking. This must always be correlated with clinical history to determine clinical relevance. A positive SPT alone does not confirm clinical allergy requiring avoidance. SPT detects Type I (IgE-mediated) hypersensitivity, not Type IV (cell-mediated/delayed) or complement-mediated responses.
citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

stem: Which medications must be withheld before skin prick testing and for how long?

choices.feedback: Inhaled corticosteroids do NOT suppress skin test reactivity and do NOT need to be withheld.

choices.text: Antihistamines for 3-7 days depending on the agent
choices.feedback: Correct! Antihistamines suppress the wheal-and-flare response → false negatives. Withdrawal times: first-gen (diphenhydramine): 3-5 days; second-gen (cetirizine, loratadine): 3-7 days; hydroxyzine: 7+ days; tricyclic antidepressants (doxepin): 7+ days. Medications that do NOT need to be stopped: systemic corticosteroids (short courses), inhaled corticosteroids, leukotriene modifiers, decongestants, beta-agonists.
Leukotriene receptor antagonists (montelukast) do NOT suppress skin prick test results and do not need to be withheld.
Only medications that block histamine H1 receptors need to be withheld. Most medications have no effect on skin testing.

explanation: First-generation antihistamines (diphenhydramine, chlorpheniramine) must be withheld for 3 days, second-generation antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine) for 3-7 days, and hydroxyzine for 7 days before SPT. Antihistamines suppress the histamine-mediated wheal response, causing false-negative results. Inhaled corticosteroids do not need to be withheld — they do not affect cutaneous mast cell reactivity. Leukotriene inhibitors (montelukast) do not significantly suppress SPT. Oral corticosteroids in short courses have minimal effect on SPT (may suppress if used >1 week at high doses). There is no need to stop all oral medications.

stem: Component-resolved diagnostics (CRD) for peanut allergy reveals high Ara h 2 IgE. What does this indicate?

choices.text: Tolerance to roasted but not raw peanut
choices.feedback: Ara h 2 is heat-stable, so patients sensitized to it typically react to both raw and roasted peanut.

choices.text: High risk of systemic/anaphylactic reaction to peanut
choices.feedback: Correct! Ara h 2 is a 2S albumin storage protein — the most predictive component for clinical peanut allergy and anaphylaxis risk. Stable to heat/digestion → triggers systemic reactions. Compare: Ara h 8 (PR-10, birch-peanut cross-reactivity → usually mild oral symptoms only), Ara h 9 (lipid transfer protein → variable severity). CRD helps distinguish true peanut allergy from cross-reactivity and guides oral food challenge decisions.
Cross-reactivity with birch pollen is associated with Ara h 8 (PR-10 protein), not Ara h 2.

choices.text: Outgrowth of peanut allergy is likely
choices.feedback: High Ara h 2 levels are associated with persistent allergy and higher reaction severity, making outgrowth less likely.

explanation: Ara h 2 is a 2S albumin storage protein in peanut that is the strongest predictor of clinical peanut allergy and systemic/anaphylactic reactions. It is a stable protein resistant to heat and digestion, meaning sensitization to Ara h 2 correlates with reactions to all forms of peanut (raw, roasted, processed). In contrast, Ara h 8 (PR-10, birch cross-reactive) predicts oral allergy syndrome only, and Ara h 9 (lipid transfer protein) has geographic variability. High Ara h 2 IgE does not predict tolerance to roasted peanut (storage proteins are heat-stable). It indicates genuine peanut allergy, not just birch cross-reactivity, and does not predict outgrowth.

stem: When is serum tryptase most useful in allergy diagnosis?

choices.text: To determine which foods to eliminate
choices.feedback: Tryptase does not identify specific allergens. It measures overall mast cell degranulation and mast cell burden.
Serum tryptase is not useful for allergic rhinitis diagnosis. Skin testing or sIgE is used.
Immunotherapy response is monitored clinically (symptom improvement) and sometimes with blocking antibodies (IgG4). Tryptase is not a monitoring tool for immunotherapy.

choices.text: To confirm anaphylaxis retrospectively and screen for mast cell disorders
choices.feedback: Correct! Serum tryptase: Acute draw (15 min–3 hours post-event) → elevated peak tryptase confirms mast cell degranulation/anaphylaxis. Baseline tryptase >20 ng/mL (or >11.5 using updated criteria) suggests systemic mastocytosis. Also elevated in: mast cell activation syndrome, some hematologic malignancies. Calculate: peak tryptase > 1.2× baseline + 2 = clinically significant. Always obtain baseline tryptase if anaphylaxis workup.

explanation: Serum tryptase is most useful for: (1) retrospective confirmation of anaphylaxis — peak tryptase drawn 1-2 hours after onset (elevated >11.4 ng/mL supports mast cell degranulation), and (2) screening for mast cell disorders (mastocytosis) when baseline tryptase is persistently elevated. Baseline tryptase >20 ng/mL warrants bone marrow biopsy for systemic mastocytosis. Tryptase does not identify specific food allergens for elimination diets. It plays no role in diagnosing seasonal rhinitis (which does not involve significant systemic mast cell degranulation). It is not used to monitor immunotherapy response.
citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

stem: Which biomarker best identifies Type 2 (T2)-high asthma amenable to biologic therapy?

choices.feedback: Procalcitonin is a marker of bacterial infection, not asthma phenotyping.
Sputum neutrophils indicate non-T2 (neutrophilic) asthma, which currently has fewer targeted biologic options.
Total IgG is not relevant to asthma phenotyping. Total IgE and allergen-specific IgE are the relevant immunoglobulin markers.

choices.text: Blood eosinophils ≥150 cells/μL and/or FeNO ≥20 ppb
choices.feedback: Correct! T2-high asthma biomarkers: blood eosinophils (≥150-300 cells/μL, threshold varies by biologic), FeNO (fractional exhaled nitric oxide, ≥20 ppb suggests eosinophilic airway inflammation), and total/specific IgE. T2-high includes: allergic asthma (IgE-driven → omalizumab), eosinophilic asthma (IL-5 → mepolizumab, benralizumab; IL-4/13 → dupilumab). T2-low asthma: neutrophilic or paucigranulocytic, fewer biologic options.

explanation: T2-high asthma amenable to biologic therapy is identified by blood eosinophils ≥150 cells/μL and/or FeNO ≥20 ppb (GINA thresholds). These biomarkers reflect IL-5-driven eosinophil production and IL-13-driven iNOS upregulation in airway epithelium, respectively. Together they predict response to biologics targeting IL-4, IL-5, IL-13, IgE, or TSLP. Procalcitonin is a bacterial sepsis marker. Sputum neutrophil counts identify neutrophilic (non-T2) inflammation. Serum IgG has no role in asthma phenotyping (IgE is relevant for allergic asthma, not IgG).

stem: A patient with severe eosinophilic asthma (blood eosinophils 500 cells/μL) fails high-dose ICS/LABA. Which biologic targets IL-5?

choices.feedback: Dupilumab targets IL-4Rα (blocking both IL-4 and IL-13). While effective in eosinophilic asthma, it does not target IL-5 specifically.
Omalizumab binds IgE and is indicated for allergic asthma with elevated IgE, not specifically for eosinophilic asthma.

Correct! Anti-IL-5 biologics for eosinophilic asthma: Mepolizumab (anti-IL-5 mAb → prevents IL-5 from binding receptor on eosinophils), Reslizumab (also anti-IL-5, IV administration), Benralizumab (anti-IL-5Rα → directly depletes eosinophils via ADCC — most rapid eosinophil reduction). All reduce exacerbations by ~50% and enable OCS sparing. Selection based on: eosinophil count, IgE level, comorbidities (nasal polyps → dupilumab preferred).
Tezepelumab targets TSLP (thymic stromal lymphopoietin), an upstream epithelial cytokine. It is effective across phenotypes but does not specifically target IL-5.

explanation: Mepolizumab (anti-IL-5) is specifically indicated for severe eosinophilic asthma uncontrolled on high-dose ICS/LABA. With blood eosinophils of 500 cells/μL, this patient has clear eosinophilic phenotype. Mepolizumab reduces exacerbations by approximately 50% and allows oral corticosteroid dose reduction. Dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα) is also effective for eosinophilic asthma but works via IL-4/IL-13, not IL-5. Omalizumab (anti-IgE) requires documented allergen sensitization and elevated IgE. Tezepelumab (anti-TSLP) is phenotype-agnostic but the question specifically asks which targets IL-5.

stem: What distinguishes T2-low asthma from T2-high asthma?

choices.text: Normal eosinophils/FeNO, neutrophilic or paucigranulocytic inflammation, and poor response to corticosteroids
choices.feedback: Correct! T2-low asthma: normal blood eosinophils (<150) and FeNO (<20 ppb), often neutrophilic sputum pattern. Associated with: obesity, smoking, occupational exposures, older onset. Poor response to ICS. Limited biologic options — tezepelumab (anti-TSLP) shows some benefit. Management focuses on: LAMA add-on, macrolides (azithromycin for neutrophilic), bronchial thermoplasty, weight loss if obese.

choices.text: T2-low asthma responds better to inhaled corticosteroids
choices.feedback: T2-LOW asthma actually responds POORLY to inhaled corticosteroids — this is one of its defining features.
Fixed obstruction (asthma-COPD overlap) can occur in both T2-high and T2-low phenotypes and is not a distinguishing feature.
Viral triggers are common in all asthma phenotypes. This does not specifically distinguish T2-low from T2-high.

explanation: T2-low asthma is characterized by normal blood eosinophils and FeNO with neutrophilic (sputum neutrophils >61%) or paucigranulocytic (few inflammatory cells of any type) inflammation. Critically, T2-low asthma responds poorly to inhaled and systemic corticosteroids, which primarily target eosinophilic inflammation. Potential drivers include obesity, smoking, infections, and neutrophilic pathways (IL-17, IL-8). T2-high asthma actually responds better to ICS. Fixed airflow obstruction can occur in both phenotypes with long-standing disease. Viral-triggered exacerbations are common across all asthma phenotypes.
citation: Asthma. StatPearls. NBK430901. [Open Access] | PMC5765385. [Open Access]

stem: FeNO is measured at 45 ppb in a patient with uncontrolled asthma on medium-dose ICS. What is the most likely interpretation?

choices.text: Ongoing eosinophilic inflammation suggesting poor ICS adherence or need for ICS step-up
choices.feedback: Correct! FeNO > 25 ppb in adults (>20 in children) indicates ongoing eosinophilic airway inflammation. In a patient on ICS with high FeNO: (1) Check adherence/inhaler technique (most common reason), (2) Check for allergen exposure driving ongoing inflammation, (3) Consider stepping up ICS or adding biologic. FeNO is suppressed by ICS when taken correctly, so persistent elevation despite ICS suggests non-adherence or refractory T2 inflammation.
FeNO ≥ 25 ppb suggests eosinophilic/T2-high inflammation, NOT non-eosinophilic.

choices.text: The patient needs antibiotics for a respiratory infection
choices.feedback: Elevated FeNO indicates eosinophilic inflammation, not bacterial infection. Procalcitonin or CRP would be more relevant for infection.
FeNO of 45 ppb is elevated and indicates ongoing T2 inflammation. Normal FeNO is < 25 ppb in adults.

explanation: FeNO of 45 ppb is elevated (normal <25 ppb) and indicates ongoing eosinophilic airway inflammation despite medium-dose ICS. The most common explanations are poor ICS adherence (most frequent cause), incorrect inhaler technique, or genuine need for ICS dose escalation/add-on therapy. This finding confirms T2/eosinophilic inflammation, not non-eosinophilic asthma. Elevated FeNO does not indicate bacterial infection requiring antibiotics. A FeNO of 45 ppb is distinctly abnormal in asthma and warrants clinical action — adherence assessment should come first, followed by step-up therapy if adherence is confirmed.

stem: Which biologic is appropriate for BOTH severe eosinophilic asthma AND comorbid chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps?

choices.feedback: Correct! Dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα) blocks both IL-4 and IL-13 → broad T2 inflammation suppression. Indicated for: moderate-severe eosinophilic asthma, chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps (CRSwNP), atopic dermatitis, eosinophilic esophagitis, and prurigo nodularis. The shared T2 pathway makes it ideal for asthma + polyps comorbidity. Reduces exacerbations, improves FEV1, shrinks polyps, and reduces need for sinus surgery.
Omalizumab treats allergic asthma and is approved for chronic urticaria, but dupilumab has stronger evidence for the nasal polyps indication.
Benralizumab (anti-IL-5Rα) effectively treats eosinophilic asthma but is not indicated for nasal polyps. Dupilumab covers both.
Tezepelumab is approved for asthma but is not currently indicated for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps.

explanation: Dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα) blocks both IL-4 and IL-13 signaling, targeting the shared receptor subunit. It is indicated for both severe eosinophilic asthma AND chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps (CRSwNP), making it uniquely appropriate for patients with both comorbidities. Clinical trials show significant reduction in both asthma exacerbations and nasal polyp size. Omalizumab (anti-IgE) is approved for allergic asthma but not specifically for CRSwNP. Benralizumab (anti-IL-5Rα) depletes eosinophils but does not have the same dual indication for nasal polyps as dupilumab. Tezepelumab is approved for severe asthma but not yet for CRSwNP.
citation: Asthma. StatPearls. NBK430901. [Open Access] | PMC9316404. [Open Access]

stem: A 55-year-old woman on propranolol develops anaphylaxis to IV contrast. After two appropriate IM epinephrine doses and 2L crystalloid, she remains hypotensive with heart rate 52. What is the next pharmacologic intervention?

choices.text: IV glucagon, which bypasses beta-adrenergic blockade via direct cAMP activation
choices.feedback: Correct! Glucagon activates adenylyl cyclase independently of the beta receptor, increasing intracellular cAMP. This provides inotropic and chronotropic support even when beta receptors are blocked.

choices.text: IV atropine to treat the bradycardia before repeating epinephrine
choices.feedback: Atropine addresses vagal-mediated bradycardia but does not overcome beta-blocker-induced resistance to epinephrine in anaphylaxis.

choices.text: IV methylprednisolone for refractory anaphylaxis from immune complex deposition
choices.feedback: Steroids have no role in acute anaphylaxis management — onset is hours, too slow for an emergency. They may help prevent biphasic reactions but do not treat acute refractory symptoms.

choices.text: Higher-dose IM epinephrine to overcome the beta-blockade
choices.feedback: Simply increasing epinephrine dose does not overcome pharmacologic beta-blockade effectively and increases risk of alpha-mediated hypertension and arrhythmias.

explanation: In beta-blocker refractory anaphylaxis (propranolol blocks both beta-1 and beta-2 receptors), IV glucagon is the next agent because it activates adenylyl cyclase and increases cAMP independent of the beta-adrenergic receptor pathway. Glucagon provides positive inotropic and chronotropic effects, and bronchodilation, bypassing the beta-blockade. IV atropine may treat bradycardia but does not address hypotension from vasodilation or bronchospasm. IV methylprednisolone has delayed onset and does not treat acute refractory anaphylaxis. Higher-dose IM epinephrine faces the same beta-receptor blockade.
citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

stem: After successful treatment of anaphylaxis with one dose of IM epinephrine, how long should the patient be monitored before discharge?

choices.text: Minimum 4-6 hours; longer observation for severe reactions, beta-blocker use, or history of biphasic reactions
1 hour is sufficient if all symptoms have resolved completely
24-hour mandatory inpatient admission for all anaphylaxis episodes regardless of severity
Observation until antihistamine effects fully resolve all cutaneous symptoms

explanation: After successful anaphylaxis treatment, patients should be monitored for a minimum of 4-6 hours, with longer observation for severe initial presentations, beta-blocker use, or history of biphasic reactions (which occur in up to 20% of cases, typically within 4-12 hours). One hour is insufficient — biphasic reactions can occur well beyond this window. Mandatory 24-hour admission for all episodes is not evidence-based and not practical for mild, rapidly resolving cases. Observation is not determined by antihistamine effect resolution — it is based on biphasic reaction risk.
citation: Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

stem: What is the mechanism of allergen immunotherapy (allergy shots)?

choices.text: Immune deviation from Th2 to Th1/Treg responses with production of blocking IgG4 antibodies
choices.feedback: Correct! Allergen immunotherapy induces: (1) Immune deviation: shifts from Th2 (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13) toward Th1 and Treg (IL-10, TGF-β). (2) Blocking antibodies: increases allergen-specific IgG4 → competes with IgE for allergen binding → prevents mast cell activation. (3) Reduced mast cell/basophil reactivity. (4) Decreased tissue eosinophilia. Clinical: reduces symptoms, medication needs, and prevents new sensitizations. Disease-modifying — effects persist years after completion.
Immunotherapy does not desensitize histamine receptors. Antihistamines block histamine receptors; immunotherapy modifies the upstream immune response.
Immunotherapy actually causes a transient INCREASE in specific IgE early on, followed by gradual decline. The key mechanism is IgG4 blocking antibodies and Treg induction.

choices.text: Suppression of all immune responses via immunosuppressive drugs
choices.feedback: Immunotherapy does not use immunosuppressive drugs. It modifies the specific allergic immune response.

explanation: Allergen immunotherapy induces immune deviation from Th2 to Th1/Treg responses. Key mechanisms include Treg-mediated IL-10 production, suppression of eosinophils and mast cells, and class switching from IgE to IgG4 blocking antibodies that compete with IgE for allergen binding. Histamine receptors are not directly desensitized by AIT. IgE is not depleted — it gradually declines over years while IgG4 rises 10-100 fold. AIT is allergen-specific immunomodulation, not broad immunosuppression via drugs.

stem: A patient receiving subcutaneous immunotherapy (SCIT) develops generalized urticaria and throat tightness 15 minutes after injection. What is the immediate treatment?

choices.feedback: Albuterol addresses bronchospasm but not the systemic anaphylaxis (hypotension, angioedema). Epinephrine is needed for the systemic reaction.
Steroids take hours to work and do not treat acute anaphylaxis. Epinephrine is the first-line treatment that must be given immediately.

choices.text: IM epinephrine, then monitor and activate emergency protocol
choices.feedback: Correct! Systemic reaction during SCIT = anaphylaxis protocol: (1) IM epinephrine immediately, (2) supine position with legs elevated, (3) supplemental oxygen, (4) IV access and fluids, (5) H1 + H2 antihistamines, (6) observe minimum 30-60 minutes after stabilization. All SCIT offices must have: epinephrine, resuscitation equipment, trained staff, 30-minute post-injection observation requirement. Dose adjustment: future injections reduced (typically back 2-3 dose levels).
Oral antihistamines are adjunctive only. This presentation requires immediate epinephrine — throat tightness suggests anaphylaxis.

explanation: Generalized urticaria with throat tightness after SCIT injection is anaphylaxis requiring immediate IM epinephrine (0.3-0.5 mg of 1:1000 concentration in the anterolateral thigh). This is the first-line treatment — delay increases mortality risk. After epinephrine, activate emergency protocol, monitor vital signs, and prepare for potential second dose if symptoms persist. Nebulized albuterol alone is insufficient — it addresses bronchospasm but not systemic anaphylaxis. IV methylprednisolone may help prevent biphasic reactions but has delayed onset and should not replace epinephrine. Oral diphenhydramine alone is inadequate for anaphylaxis — antihistamines are adjunctive, not primary treatment.
citation: Allergy Immunotherapy. StatPearls. NBK535367. [Open Access] | Type I Hypersensitivity. StatPearls. NBK560561. [Open Access]

stem: How does sublingual immunotherapy (SLIT) differ from subcutaneous immunotherapy (SCIT)?

choices.text: SLIT can be administered at home after the first dose, with lower systemic reaction risk
choices.feedback: Correct! SLIT (sublingual tablets/drops): administered at home daily after supervised first dose in clinic. Lower anaphylaxis risk than SCIT (no deaths reported in US clinical trials). Available SLIT: grass (Grastek), ragweed (Ragwitek), dust mite (Odactra). SCIT advantages: broader allergen combinations, possibly higher efficacy, more evidence for multiple allergens. SLIT advantages: home administration, better adherence for some, lower systemic reaction risk. Both are disease-modifying.

choices.text: SLIT is more effective than SCIT for all allergens
choices.feedback: Head-to-head comparisons generally show SCIT has somewhat greater efficacy, though SLIT has sufficient evidence for key allergens.

choices.text: SLIT requires weekly clinic visits like SCIT
choices.feedback: A major advantage of SLIT is home administration — no weekly clinic visits needed (unlike SCIT which requires in-office injections).

choices.text: SLIT works only for food allergies
choices.feedback: SLIT is available for environmental allergens (grass, ragweed, dust mite). Oral immunotherapy (OIT) is used for food allergies — a related but distinct approach.

explanation: SLIT can be self-administered at home after the initial supervised dose in the clinician's office, offering greater convenience than SCIT (which requires every injection in a medical setting with 30-minute observation). SLIT also has a significantly lower risk of systemic reactions and anaphylaxis. SLIT is not universally more effective than SCIT. SLIT does not require weekly clinic visits — daily home administration is the standard. SLIT is available for aeroallergens (grass pollen, ragweed, dust mite), not exclusively food allergies.

stem: What is the typical duration of allergen immunotherapy to achieve lasting disease modification?

choices.feedback: Unlike many chronic disease treatments, immunotherapy achieves lasting modification — most patients maintain benefit after 3-5 years of treatment.
12 months is insufficient for lasting disease modification. At least 3 years is needed based on clinical trial evidence.
6 months is typically still in the build-up phase. Insufficient for disease modification.

Correct! Recommended duration: 3-5 years of maintenance therapy. Evidence shows: sustained clinical benefit persists 7-12+ years after discontinuation (disease-modifying). Additional benefits: prevents new allergen sensitizations and reduces risk of allergic rhinitis progressing to asthma in children (preventive effect). Shorter courses (<3 years) may provide temporary relief but do not achieve lasting immune modification. SCIT: build-up phase (weekly) + maintenance (monthly); SLIT: daily throughout.

explanation: Three to five years of maintenance allergen immunotherapy is the recommended duration for lasting disease modification. This timeframe allows sufficient immune remodeling (Treg expansion, IgG4 production, gradual IgE decline) to produce sustained clinical benefit persisting years after discontinuation. Lifelong therapy is not required. Twelve months or 6 months is insufficient — the build-up phase alone takes 3-6 months, and sustained tolerance requires years of maintenance.

stem: Which condition is the ONLY current established indication for venom immunotherapy?

choices.feedback: Large local reactions to fire ants do not require VIT. Systemic reactions do.
There is no established immunotherapy for mosquito allergy. Mosquito allergy is managed with avoidance, antihistamines, and topical treatments.
Chronic urticaria is treated with antihistamines and omalizumab, not venom immunotherapy.

choices.text: Hymenoptera venom anaphylaxis confirmed by positive skin test or sIgE
choices.feedback: Correct! Venom immunotherapy (VIT) is indicated for patients with a history of systemic (anaphylactic) reaction to Hymenoptera sting AND positive venom skin test or serum-specific IgE. Covers: honeybee, yellow jacket, hornet, wasp, fire ant. VIT is 97-98% effective at preventing future systemic reactions (most effective form of immunotherapy). Duration: typically 5 years, possibly lifelong for highest-risk patients (prior severe anaphylaxis, elevated baseline tryptase, mast cell disorders).

explanation: Venom immunotherapy (VIT) is specifically indicated for patients with systemic (anaphylactic) reactions to Hymenoptera stings (bees, wasps, hornets, yellow jackets, fire ants) confirmed by positive venom skin testing or elevated venom-specific IgE. VIT reduces the risk of future anaphylaxis from approximately 60% to less than 5%. Large local reactions alone (even if extensive) are not an indication for VIT — these carry very low risk of future systemic reactions. Mosquito bite hypersensitivity does not have an established VIT protocol. Chronic idiopathic urticaria is not venom-related and is not treated with VIT.

stem: A patient prescribed a dry powder inhaler (DPI) demonstrates poor technique. Which error is UNIQUE to DPI devices?

choices.feedback: Breath-holding after inhalation (5-10 seconds) is important for both MDIs and DPIs. This is not unique to DPI technique.

choices.text: Exhaling into the device before inhalation
choices.feedback: While exhaling into some DPIs can clump the powder with moisture, the most critical and unique DPI error is insufficient inspiratory flow rate.
Shaking is required for MDIs (to suspend medication in propellant). DPIs do not require shaking — they are breath-activated powders.

Correct! DPIs are breath-activated — they require a fast, forceful inhalation (peak inspiratory flow ≥target rate depending on device) to deaggregate and disperse the powder. This is the OPPOSITE of MDI technique (slow, steady inhalation). Patients with severe obstruction, young children, or elderly may not generate sufficient flow → poor drug delivery. DPIs should NOT be used with spacers. Advantage: no propellant, no coordination needed; Disadvantage: flow-dependent delivery.

explanation: Insufficient inspiratory flow rate is the error unique to DPI devices. DPIs require a quick, forceful inhalation (≥30-60 L/min) to deaggregate the powder and generate a respirable aerosol — without adequate flow, the drug remains in the device or deposits in the oropharynx. This error does not apply to MDIs or nebulizers, which generate aerosol independently of patient effort. Not holding breath after inhalation is a common error across all inhaler types. Exhaling into a DPI before inhalation introduces moisture that clumps the powder — important but also relevant to other devices. Failure to shake applies to MDIs, not DPIs.

stem: Which inhaler device is most appropriate for a 3-year-old with asthma?

choices.feedback: DPIs require sufficient inspiratory flow (>target rate) that young children cannot generate. Inappropriate for children under ~6.
Soft mist inhalers are approved for patients ≥6 years with certain medications. Not appropriate as first choice for a 3-year-old.
Breath-actuated MDIs require a minimum inspiratory flow to trigger. Toddlers cannot reliably trigger these devices.

choices.text: MDI with spacer and face mask
choices.feedback: Correct! For children <4 years: MDI + spacer + face mask (tight seal over nose and mouth). For children 4-6: MDI + spacer with mouthpiece (can maintain mouth seal). For children >6: MDI + spacer, or DPI if adequate inspiratory effort. Nebulizers are an alternative but MDI + spacer is equally effective for most situations, more portable, and faster. Key technique: apply mask gently (no crying — crying reduces deposition), 5-6 tidal breaths through spacer after each actuation.

explanation: An MDI with spacer and face mask is the most appropriate device for a 3-year-old with asthma. Young children cannot coordinate the breath-actuated mechanisms required by DPIs or breath-actuated MDIs, and cannot generate the forceful inspiratory flow DPIs require (≥30 L/min). The face mask ensures effective drug delivery during tidal breathing. Soft mist inhalers (Respimat) require slow, coordinated inhalation that is difficult for a 3-year-old. Breath-actuated MDIs without spacers require inspiratory triggering beyond a young child's capability.

stem: What is the most common error in MDI technique?

choices.text: Inhaling through the nose instead of mouth
choices.feedback: While nasal breathing would reduce drug delivery, the most common and impactful error is actuation-inhalation timing (coordination).

choices.text: Poor coordination between actuation and inhalation
choices.feedback: Correct! The most common MDI error is poor hand-breath coordination — actuating the canister at the wrong time relative to inhalation. Other common errors: not shaking before use, inhaling too fast (should be slow and deep over 3-5 seconds), not holding breath (should hold 5-10 seconds after inhalation), not waiting between puffs (wait 30-60 seconds). Spacers eliminate the coordination problem. Studies show 50-80% of patients use MDIs incorrectly without training.

choices.text: Storing the inhaler in the wrong temperature
choices.feedback: Storage temperature affects MDI stability but is not a technique error during use.
Handedness does not significantly affect MDI delivery. The most common errors involve timing and breathing pattern.

explanation: Poor coordination between actuation (pressing the canister) and inhalation is the most common MDI technique error. Patients frequently actuate too early, too late, or fail to synchronize the press-and-breathe maneuver, resulting in most medication depositing in the mouth and throat rather than reaching the lungs. This is why spacers are recommended — they eliminate the need for precise coordination. Inhaling through the nose is an error but less common. Inhaler storage temperature is rarely a practical issue. Hand dominance is irrelevant to technique.

stem: After using an ICS inhaler, what instruction reduces the risk of oral candidiasis?

choices.feedback: Antifungal prophylaxis is not recommended routinely for ICS users. Simple technique measures are effective.

choices.text: Brush teeth immediately before using the inhaler
choices.feedback: Brushing before use does not prevent ICS-related thrush. The rinse should be AFTER use to remove deposited steroid.

choices.text: Rinse mouth with water and spit (do not swallow) after each ICS use
choices.feedback: Correct! Mouth rinsing after ICS reduces oropharyngeal steroid deposition → lowers risk of oral candidiasis (thrush) and dysphonia. Spit, don't swallow (swallowing increases GI absorption → systemic effects). Additional measures: use a spacer (reduces oropharyngeal deposition by ~50%), use lowest effective ICS dose, good dental hygiene. If thrush develops despite measures: oral nystatin suspension or fluconazole, consider spacer addition or ICS formulation change.

choices.text: Use the inhaler only at bedtime
choices.feedback: Timing of ICS use does not significantly affect thrush risk. Mouth rinsing technique is what matters.

explanation: Rinsing the mouth with water and spitting (not swallowing) after each ICS use removes residual corticosteroid from the oropharynx, reducing the risk of oral candidiasis and dysphonia. This simple measure, combined with spacer use, significantly decreases local ICS side effects. Daily prophylactic antifungals are unnecessary and add drug burden. Brushing teeth before (not after) using the inhaler does not remove post-inhalation drug residue. Using ICS only at bedtime does not prevent oropharyngeal deposition — the drug still contacts the throat regardless of timing.

stem: What is the most common adverse reaction during IVIG infusion?

choices.feedback: IVIG does not typically cause neutropenia. It more commonly causes rate-related infusion reactions.

choices.text: Rate-related infusion reactions: headache, fever, chills, myalgias
choices.feedback: Correct! Most common: rate-related reactions (5-15% of infusions) — headache, fever, chills, myalgias, nausea, back pain, fatigue. Management: slow infusion rate, premedicate (acetaminophen, diphenhydramine, sometimes low-dose hydrocortisone). Serious but rare: aseptic meningitis, thromboembolic events (especially in elderly, immobilized, or hypercoagulable), renal failure (especially sucrose-containing products), hemolytic anemia (anti-A/anti-B antibodies in IVIG), TRALI.
Renal failure is a rare serious AE, especially with sucrose-stabilized products (osmotic nephropathy). Use sucrose-free formulations in at-risk patients. This is NOT the most common reaction.
True anaphylaxis to IVIG is rare (most common in IgA-deficient patients with anti-IgA antibodies). Infusion-related reactions are much more common.

explanation: Rate-related infusion reactions are the most common adverse reactions during IVIG infusion, presenting as headache, fever, chills, and myalgias. These occur due to cytokine activation and immune complex formation and are managed by slowing or temporarily stopping the infusion, then resuming at a lower rate. Premedication with acetaminophen, diphenhydramine, and/or hydrocortisone reduces incidence. More serious reactions (anaphylaxis, aseptic meningitis, thromboembolism) are much rarer.

stem: How does SCIG (subcutaneous immunoglobulin) differ from IVIG?

choices.text: SCIG is self-administered at home with steadier IgG levels
choices.feedback: Correct! SCIG advantages: home self-administration (weekly/biweekly), steady-state IgG levels (no peak-trough fluctuations — IgG varies only ~5% vs ~35% with IVIG), significantly fewer systemic side effects (headaches, fatigue), no IV access needed. Disadvantages: local site reactions (swelling, redness — usually mild, improve with time), smaller volume per site (multiple sites may be needed), more frequent administration. Equivalent infection protection to IVIG. Increasingly preferred for quality of life.

choices.text: SCIG provides higher peak IgG levels than IVIG
choices.feedback: IVIG achieves higher peak levels immediately post-infusion. SCIG provides more stable, steady-state levels without the peak-trough pattern.

choices.text: SCIG is given monthly in a clinic like IVIG
choices.feedback: SCIG is typically self-administered at home weekly or biweekly, not monthly in a clinic.

choices.text: SCIG is only available for XLA, not CVID
choices.feedback: SCIG is approved for all primary immunodeficiencies requiring IgG replacement, including both CVID and XLA.

explanation: SCIG differs from IVIG in route (subcutaneous vs intravenous), setting (home vs infusion center), frequency (weekly-biweekly vs monthly), and IgG kinetics (steady levels vs peak-trough). SCIG is self-administered, allows greater patient independence, and produces more stable IgG levels. Systemic adverse effects are significantly lower with SCIG. Local site reactions (swelling, erythema) are the main SCIG side effect. Both deliver equivalent total IgG doses when properly dosed.

stem: Why must IgA levels be checked before starting IVIG in a patient with suspected immunodeficiency?

choices.text: To determine the correct IVIG dose
choices.feedback: IVIG dosing is based on weight and target trough IgG levels, not IgA levels.
IVIG efficacy is monitored by trough IgG levels and clinical response (infection frequency), not IgA levels.

choices.text: IgA deficiency contraindicates all immunoglobulin therapy
choices.feedback: IgA deficiency does NOT absolutely contraindicate immunoglobulin therapy — it requires using IgA-depleted products and careful monitoring. Many IgA-deficient patients receive IVIG safely.

choices.text: Patients with selective IgA deficiency may have anti-IgA antibodies that cause anaphylaxis to IgA-containing IVIG products
choices.feedback: Correct! Selective IgA deficiency (most common PID, ~1:500) → some develop anti-IgA IgG/IgE antibodies → anaphylactic reaction when exposed to IgA in standard IVIG/blood products. Management: use IgA-depleted IVIG products (e.g., Gammagard S/D) or SCIG (lower IgA content per dose, slower absorption → reduced reaction risk). Screen IgA levels before first infusion. Most IgA-deficient patients tolerate IVIG fine (anti-IgA antibodies are uncommon), but screening prevents rare fatal reactions.

explanation: IgA levels must be checked before starting IVIG because selective IgA deficiency (the most common primary immunodeficiency, ~1:500) predisposes to anti-IgA antibody development. These antibodies can cause anaphylaxis when the patient receives IgA-containing immunoglobulin products. Identified IgA-deficient patients should receive IgA-depleted products or SCIG. Anti-IgA antibodies can be IgG class (forming immune complexes) or IgE class (triggering mast cell degranulation), both causing systemic reactions.

stem: At what dose range is IVIG considered immunomodulatory rather than replacement?

choices.feedback: Correct! IVIG dosing: Replacement: 400-600 mg/kg every 3-4 weeks (maintain trough IgG). Immunomodulatory: 1-2 g/kg (high-dose) for autoimmune/inflammatory conditions. Immunomodulatory indications: ITP (immune thrombocytopenia), Guillain-Barré syndrome, Kawasaki disease, chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP), myasthenia gravis exacerbation. Mechanism at high dose: Fc receptor blockade, anti-idiotypic antibodies, complement scavenging, cytokine modulation.
200-400 mg/kg monthly is the standard REPLACEMENT dose for primary immunodeficiency.
5 g/kg exceeds standard immunomodulatory dosing and would carry unacceptable side effect risk (volume overload, thromboembolic events).
50-100 mg/kg weekly is closer to a SCIG replacement regimen, not immunomodulatory dosing.

explanation:
References: Perez EE, et al. Immunoglobulin Use in Human Disease. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2017;139(3):S1-S46. [Open Access]

Reference: Immunoglobulin Therapy - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK513460/ (StatPearls, 2025)
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with nasal congestion, clear rhinorrhea, and sneezing that worsen in spring and fall. She has bluish discoloration under her eyes and a transverse nasal crease. Nasal examination shows pale, boggy turbinates. Which immunoglobulin mediates her condition?

choices.feedback: IgG mediates type II and III hypersensitivity reactions. Allergic rhinitis is type I (IgE-mediated).
IgA is the predominant mucosal immunoglobulin but does not mediate allergic rhinitis.
IgM is the first antibody produced in a primary immune response and does not mediate allergic rhinitis.

Allergic rhinitis is an IgE-mediated type I hypersensitivity reaction. Allergen cross-links IgE on mast cells → degranulation → histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins. Pale turbinates, allergic shiners, and the nasal salute crease are classic exam findings.

explanation: References: Allergic Rhinitis. StatPearls. NBK538186. [Open Access]
auditNote: Batch 27: Verified accurate

stem: A patient with chronic allergic rhinitis is started on intranasal corticosteroids. Which mechanism best explains their therapeutic effect?

choices.feedback: This describes montelukast, a second-line option for allergic rhinitis.
This describes cromolyn sodium, which prevents mast cell degranulation but is less effective than intranasal corticosteroids.

Intranasal corticosteroids are first-line for moderate-persistent allergic rhinitis. They suppress IL-4, IL-5, IL-13, reduce eosinophil recruitment, and decrease mucosal edema. They address both early and late-phase allergic responses.
This describes antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine), not corticosteroids.

explanation: Best answer: Suppression of inflammatory cytokines and reduction of mucosal eosinophilic infiltration.
auditNote: Batch 27: Verified accurate

stem: A 35-year-old man with perennial allergic rhinitis fails to improve despite combination therapy with intranasal corticosteroids and oral antihistamines. Skin prick testing reveals sensitivity to dust mites and cat dander. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.feedback: Systemic corticosteroids should be avoided long-term for allergic rhinitis due to side effects. Immunotherapy addresses the underlying immune mechanism.
Surgery may help with structural obstruction but does not address the immunologic basis of allergic rhinitis.
Ipratropium addresses rhinorrhea only and does not treat the underlying allergic mechanism.

Allergen immunotherapy (SCIT or SLIT) is indicated for patients with moderate-severe allergic rhinitis refractory to pharmacotherapy. It works by shifting immune response from Th2 to Th1, increasing blocking IgG4 antibodies, and inducing regulatory T cells. Duration is typically 3-5 years.

explanation: References: Allergic Rhinitis. StatPearls. NBK538186. [Open Access]
auditNote: Batch 28: Verified accurate

stem: Which of the following is the most common comorbidity associated with persistent allergic rhinitis?

choices.feedback: Nasal polyps are more associated with non-allergic rhinitis, aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease, and chronic rhinosinusitis.
While GERD can cause chronic cough and laryngeal symptoms, it is not the most common comorbidity of allergic rhinitis.

Up to 40% of allergic rhinitis patients have comorbid asthma (the 'united airway' concept). The nasal and bronchial mucosa share similar inflammatory pathways. Treating allergic rhinitis improves asthma control. Other comorbidities include sinusitis, otitis media, and sleep disturbance.
Nasal obstruction can worsen OSA, but asthma is the most commonly associated comorbidity with allergic rhinitis.

explanation: References: Allergic Rhinitis. StatPearls. NBK538186. [Open Access]
auditNote: Batch 28: Verified accurate

stem: A patient asks about the difference between seasonal and perennial allergic rhinitis. Which allergen is most commonly responsible for perennial symptoms?

choices.feedback: Perennial allergic rhinitis is most commonly caused by dust mites, pet dander, cockroach allergens, and mold. Seasonal rhinitis is driven by tree, grass, and weed pollens with predictable temporal patterns. Dust mite allergens (Der p 1, Der f 1) are the most common perennial trigger worldwide.
Birch pollen causes seasonal (spring) allergic rhinitis.
Grass pollen causes seasonal (spring/summer) allergic rhinitis.
Ragweed is a fall seasonal allergen, not a perennial trigger.

explanation: References: Allergic Rhinitis. StatPearls. NBK538186. [Open Access]
auditNote: Batch 28: Verified accurate

stem: A 22-year-old woman develops generalized urticaria, wheezing, and hypotension within minutes of receiving IV penicillin. Her blood pressure is 70/40 mmHg. What is the first-line treatment?

choices.feedback: IM epinephrine is the FIRST-LINE treatment for anaphylaxis. It acts on alpha-1 receptors (vasoconstriction, reduces edema), beta-1 (increases cardiac output), and beta-2 (bronchodilation, decreases mast cell mediator release). Must be given IM in the anterolateral thigh for rapid absorption. Delay in epinephrine is the #1 factor in anaphylaxis fatalities.
Corticosteroids may help prevent biphasic reactions but have delayed onset (4-6 hours) and are NOT effective for acute anaphylaxis management.
Antihistamines are adjunctive only—they treat urticaria/pruritus but do NOT reverse hypotension or airway compromise. Never use as first-line for anaphylaxis.
Albuterol is adjunctive for bronchospasm but does not address hypotension or systemic anaphylaxis. Epinephrine must come first.

explanation: Intramuscular epinephrine (0.3-0.5 mg of 1:1000 concentration) in the anterolateral thigh is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis. Epinephrine acts on alpha-1 receptors to increase peripheral vascular resistance (reversing hypotension), on beta-1 receptors to increase cardiac output, and on beta-2 receptors to cause bronchodilation and suppress further mediator release from mast cells. IM injection in the vastus lateralis provides more rapid and reliable absorption than subcutaneous administration. IV methylprednisolone, diphenhydramine, and albuterol are adjunctive therapies but should never delay or replace epinephrine. Half of anaphylaxis-related fatalities occur in the first hour, making rapid epinephrine administration critical.

references.supportingQuote: Rapid treatment should be initiated with intramuscular epinephrine if any of these symptoms are present

stem: A patient treated for anaphylaxis in the ED improves with epinephrine, but 6 hours later develops recurrent hypotension and dyspnea. What is this phenomenon called?

choices.feedback: Biphasic anaphylaxis occurs in up to 20% of cases, typically 1-72 hours after initial resolution (most within 8-10 hours). Risk factors include severe initial reaction, delayed epinephrine, and need for multiple epinephrine doses. This is why patients should be observed for at least 4-6 hours (some guidelines say 24 hours for severe reactions).
Serum sickness is a type III hypersensitivity reaction occurring 1-2 weeks after drug exposure, not hours.
Anaphylactoid reactions are non-IgE mediated (direct mast cell activation) and present similarly to anaphylaxis but are not a recurrence pattern.
Rebound bronchospasm relates to short-acting bronchodilator use, not systemic anaphylaxis recurrence.

explanation: Biphasic anaphylaxis occurs in up to 20% of patients, with recurrent symptoms peaking 8 to 11 hours after the initial reaction, even after successful initial treatment. While clinically significant in approximately 4-5% of diagnosed anaphylaxis cases, this second reaction can be fatal. Risk factors for biphasic reactions include severity of the initial reaction, persistence of symptoms, and need for more than one dose of epinephrine. This is why patients should be observed for at least 4-6 hours after an anaphylactic episode, and high-risk patients should be observed even longer. Serum sickness is a delayed Type III hypersensitivity reaction occurring days to weeks after exposure. Anaphylactoid reaction is an outdated term now consolidated under "anaphylaxis." Rebound bronchospasm describes isolated airway reactivity, not a systemic recurrence.

references.supportingQuote: Anaphylactic reactions can also occur biphasically in up to 20% of cases with recurrence peaking 8 to 11 hours after the initial reaction

stem: Which laboratory test, when drawn within 1-2 hours of a suspected anaphylactic event, best supports the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Total IgE indicates atopic predisposition but does not confirm acute anaphylaxis.

Tryptase is released from mast cell degranulation and peaks 60-90 minutes after anaphylaxis onset. A level >11.5 ng/mL supports the diagnosis. It is particularly useful when the clinical picture is ambiguous. Serial levels (at onset, 1-2 hours, and 24-hour baseline) improve diagnostic sensitivity.
CRP is a nonspecific inflammatory marker that does not distinguish anaphylaxis from other conditions.
Histamine has a very short half-life (minutes) making it impractical for clinical use. Tryptase is more stable and clinically useful.

explanation: Serum tryptase is the preferred confirmatory biomarker for anaphylaxis when drawn within 1-3 hours of symptom onset. Tryptase is released from mast cell degranulation and remains elevated for several hours, providing a practical diagnostic window. A level >11.4 ng/mL or >1.2x baseline + 2 ng/mL supports anaphylaxis. Serum histamine, while elevated during anaphylaxis, peaks within minutes and has a half-life of only about 1-2 minutes, making it impractical to measure in most clinical scenarios. Serum IgE reflects sensitization but does not confirm an acute event. CRP is a nonspecific inflammatory marker with no role in acute anaphylaxis diagnosis. Baseline tryptase should also be measured later to screen for underlying mast cell disorders.

references.supportingQuote: Serum tryptase can be considered for confirmation of an anaphylactic episode as it remains elevated for several hours

stem: A patient on a beta-blocker presents with anaphylaxis that is refractory to multiple doses of epinephrine. Which medication should be considered next?

choices.feedback: Phenylephrine is a pure alpha-agonist and may increase blood pressure but lacks the beta-2 bronchodilatory effects needed in anaphylaxis.
Atropine treats bradycardia but does not address the bronchospasm or vasodilation of anaphylaxis.
Vasopressin may be used as a pressor in refractory shock but does not specifically address beta-blocker antagonism in anaphylaxis.

Glucagon bypasses beta-adrenergic blockade by activating adenylyl cyclase independently of beta receptors. It increases cardiac output and decreases bronchospasm. Dose: 1-IV over 5 minutes. Beta-blockers make anaphylaxis more severe and epinephrine-resistant because they block the therapeutic beta-agonist effects of epinephrine.

explanation: Glucagon is recommended for patients on beta-blockers who have anaphylaxis refractory to epinephrine. Beta-blockers blunt the effects of epinephrine by occupying beta-adrenergic receptors. Glucagon bypasses beta-receptors entirely by directly activating adenylyl cyclase, increasing intracellular cyclic AMP (cAMP) in the myocardium. This produces positive inotropic and chronotropic effects independent of the adrenergic pathway. The recommended IV dose is 1-5 mg in adults, administered over 5 minutes. Common side effects include nausea and vomiting, so airway protection is important. Phenylephrine is a pure alpha-agonist and does not address bronchospasm. Atropine treats bradycardia but not the distributive shock mechanism. Vasopressin may be used as a second-line vasopressor but does not specifically address beta-blockade.

references.supportingQuote: Glucagon is the reversal agent for beta-blockers and can be used as such if needed in cases where anaphylaxis is resistant to treatment in patients with beta-blockade
Glucagon is effective in treating refractory anaphylaxis that does not respond to epinephrine

stem: According to diagnostic criteria, which scenario meets the definition of anaphylaxis?

choices.text: Localized swelling at the site of a bee sting
choices.feedback: A large local reaction is not anaphylaxis. Anaphylaxis requires systemic involvement beyond the sting site.

choices.text: Isolated urticaria and angioedema without systemic symptoms
choices.feedback: Urticaria/angioedema alone without respiratory or cardiovascular involvement does not meet anaphylaxis criteria.

choices.text: Acute onset of urticaria with hypotension after known allergen exposure
choices.feedback: Anaphylaxis criteria: (1) acute onset of skin/mucosal involvement + respiratory compromise OR hypotension, OR (2) two or more of: skin, respiratory, GI, or cardiovascular involvement after likely allergen exposure, OR (3) hypotension alone after known allergen. Skin findings are present in ~80% of cases but are NOT required for diagnosis.
Vasovagal syncope causes bradycardia and pallor, not tachycardia, urticaria, or bronchospasm.

explanation: Anaphylaxis is diagnosed clinically using consensus criteria. One key criterion is: known allergen exposure plus involvement of 2 or more body systems. In this case, urticaria (integumentary) plus hypotension (cardiovascular) after a known allergen satisfies this requirement. A third diagnostic scenario also qualifies: known allergen exposure plus hypotension alone (systolic <90 mmHg or >30% decrease from baseline). A localized sting reaction is not anaphylaxis because it involves only one site without systemic features. Isolated urticaria and angioedema without respiratory or cardiovascular compromise does not meet anaphylaxis criteria. Vasovagal syncope is neurally mediated and lacks urticaria, angioedema, or bronchospasm — key distinguishing features from anaphylaxis.

references.supportingQuote: Known exposure to an antigen and hypotension or likely exposure and symptoms involving 2 or more body systems

stem: A 6-month-old infant presents with erythematous, weeping, crusted plaques on the face and extensor surfaces of the extremities. The mother reports intense itching. Which cytokine pathway is most central to the pathogenesis of this condition?

choices.feedback: These innate cytokines contribute to inflammation broadly but are not the central pathway in atopic dermatitis.
Th17 pathway is central to psoriasis pathogenesis, not atopic dermatitis.
Th1 cytokines are involved in contact dermatitis and psoriasis, not acute atopic dermatitis (though chronic AD lesions may show Th1 involvement).

Atopic dermatitis is driven by Th2 cytokines: IL-4 and IL-13 promote IgE class switching and impair filaggrin expression (skin barrier dysfunction). IL-5 recruits eosinophils. IL-31 mediates pruritus. In infants, lesions classically affect the face and extensors; in older children/adults, flexural distribution predominates.

explanation: References: Atopic Dermatitis. StatPearls. NBK448071. [Open Access]
auditNote: Batch 28: Verified accurate

stem: Which genetic mutation is most strongly associated with atopic dermatitis and explains the impaired skin barrier?

choices.feedback: Filaggrin is essential for epidermal barrier integrity—it aggregates keratin filaments and its breakdown products form natural moisturizing factor. FLG mutations are the strongest genetic risk factor for AD, present in ~30% of patients. Impaired barrier → increased transepidermal water loss and allergen penetration, driving the Th2 inflammatory cascade.
Keratin 14 mutations cause epidermolysis bullosa simplex, not atopic dermatitis.
CARD15/NOD2 mutations are associated with Crohn's disease, not atopic dermatitis.
HLA-B27 is associated with ankylosing spondylitis and seronegative spondyloarthropathies.

explanation: Key detail: of-function mutation.
auditNote: Batch 28: Verified accurate

stem: A 4-year-old child with moderate atopic dermatitis has failed adequate emollient therapy and low-potency topical corticosteroids. What is the most appropriate next step?

choices.feedback: Treatment follows a stepwise approach: (1) emollients + trigger avoidance, (2) low-potency TCS (face/folds), (3) medium-potency TCS (body), (4) calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus/pimecrolimus) for steroid-sparing, (5) dupilumab for moderate-severe refractory disease. Medium-potency TCS is appropriate for body lesions when low-potency fails.
Dupilumab (anti-IL-4R alpha) is indicated for moderate-severe AD refractory to topical therapies, but would be premature before optimizing topical management.
Immunotherapy targets IgE-mediated conditions (rhinitis, venom allergy). Its role in AD is limited and not standard of care.
Cyclosporine is reserved for severe refractory AD after topical therapies have failed. It carries significant toxicity risks.

explanation: Key detail: potency topical corticosteroid for body lesions.
auditNote: Batch 28: Verified accurate

stem: A child with severe atopic dermatitis develops grouped vesicles on an erythematous base within areas of eczematous skin. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Contact dermatitis causes well-demarcated patches/plaques but not the characteristic grouped vesicles of HSV.

Eczema herpeticum is a medical emergency caused by HSV dissemination in atopic skin. It presents with monomorphic punched-out erosions or grouped vesicles in areas of eczema. The impaired skin barrier in AD allows HSV to spread widely. Treatment: IV acyclovir. Must distinguish from bacterial superinfection (impetigo), which shows honey-crusted lesions.
Molluscum presents as dome-shaped, umbilicated papules, not grouped vesicles. Though common in AD patients.
S. aureus superinfection (impetigo) shows honey-crusted lesions, not grouped vesicles on erythematous base.

explanation: References: Atopic Dermatitis. StatPearls. NBK448071. [Open Access]
auditNote: Batch 28: Verified accurate

stem: The 'atopic march' refers to the natural progression of atopic diseases. Which sequence correctly describes this progression?

choices.text: Allergic rhinitis → asthma → food allergy → atopic dermatitis
choices.feedback: Allergic rhinitis typically develops after AD, not before.

choices.text: Food allergy → atopic dermatitis → asthma → allergic rhinitis
choices.feedback: While food allergy can occur early, AD typically precedes food allergy development in the atopic march.

choices.text: Asthma → allergic rhinitis → atopic dermatitis → food allergy
choices.feedback: This is the reverse of the typical atopic march. AD is usually the first manifestation.

choices.text: Atopic dermatitis → food allergy → allergic rhinitis → asthma
choices.feedback: The atopic march describes the typical temporal progression: AD often appears first in infancy, followed by food allergy, then allergic rhinitis and asthma in childhood. The epicutaneous sensitization theory suggests that allergen exposure through impaired skin barrier (AD) drives systemic IgE sensitization, leading to subsequent allergic diseases.

explanation: References: Atopic Dermatitis. StatPearls. NBK448071. [Open Access]
auditNote: Batch 28: Verified accurate

stem: A 45-year-old healthcare worker develops well-demarcated, erythematous, vesicular plaques on her hands 48 hours after wearing new latex gloves. What type of hypersensitivity reaction is this?

choices.feedback: Type III reactions involve immune complex deposition (e.g., serum sickness, Arthus reaction).
Type II reactions involve IgG/IgM antibodies against cell surface antigens (e.g., hemolytic anemia).

Allergic contact dermatitis is a T-cell-mediated type IV hypersensitivity reaction. The delay (24-72 hours) reflects the time needed for antigen-presenting Langerhans cells to process hapten-protein complexes and activate sensitized T cells. Distinct from type I latex allergy (immediate IgE-mediated urticaria/anaphylaxis).
Type I latex allergy causes immediate urticaria, angioedema, or anaphylaxis—not delayed vesicular dermatitis.

explanation: References: Contact Dermatitis. StatPearls. NBK532866. [Open Access]
auditNote: Batch 28: Verified accurate

stem: Which diagnostic test is the gold standard for confirming allergic contact dermatitis?

choices.feedback: Serum IgE testing identifies type I allergen sensitization, not T-cell-mediated contact allergy.
Skin prick testing detects type I (IgE-mediated) allergy, not type IV contact sensitization.
Biopsy shows spongiotic dermatitis but cannot identify the specific causative allergen.

Patch testing applies suspected allergens to the skin under occlusion for 48 hours, then reads at 48 and 96 hours. Positive results show erythema, papules, and vesicles at the application site. The standard series includes nickel, fragrance mix, formaldehyde, rubber chemicals, and preservatives. It identifies type IV sensitizers.

stem: What is the most common contact allergen in North America?

choices.feedback: Formaldehyde is a common allergen in cosmetics and textile finishes but is less prevalent than nickel.
Urushiol causes the most common plant-related contact dermatitis but nickel is the most common overall contact allergen identified on patch testing.

Nickel is the #1 contact allergen worldwide, found in jewelry, belt buckles, cell phones, and medical devices. Prevalence is ~15-20% in women. Other top allergens: fragrance mix, balsam of Peru, formaldehyde, and neomycin. Nickel allergy presents as eczematous dermatitis at sites of metal contact.
Latex allergy has decreased significantly due to reduced use of powdered latex gloves.
