stem: A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "I keep having episodes where I flush bright red, feel like I'm going to pass out, and get terrible diarrhea. My doctor found my tryptase is high."Vital SignsBP 118/72HR 82RR 14Temp 98.6°FSpO₂ 98% A patient with recurrent flushing, diarrhea, and a baseline tryptase of 28 ng/mL (normal <11) should have which test?

choices.text: Bone marrow biopsy with KIT mutation testing

explanation:
Correct Answer: Correct! Elevated baseline tryptase >20 ng/mL suggests systemic mastocytosis. Bone marrow biopsy is gold standard, looking for abnormal mast cell aggregates and KIT D816V mutation.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Allergy testing won't diagnose mastocytosis.
- May be done to look for organomegaly but won't diagnose mastocytosis.
- This tests for pheochromocytoma - should be considered but bone marrow is priority given high tryptase.

Evidence: Consensus on Mast Cell Disorders (ECNM/AIM) - Valent P, et al. Int Arch Allergy Immunol. 2020;181(1):1-10

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Allergy skin testing: Allergy testing won't diagnose mastocytosis.
- CT scan of abdomen: May be done to look for organomegaly but won't diagnose mastocytosis.
- 24-hour urine catecholamines only: This tests for pheochromocytoma - should be considered but bone marrow is priority given high tryptase.

Reference: Consensus on Mast Cell Disorders (ECNM/AIM) - https://www.karger.com/ [Accessed 2025]
Reference: Allergy-immunology - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: An 8-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "Our son had hives and vomiting after eating something with peanuts. We need to know if he's really allergic."Vital SignsBP 98/62HR 88RR 18Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 99% An 8-year-old had hives and vomiting within 30 minutes of eating peanut butter. Peanut skin test is 12mm wheal. What is the MOST accurate statement?

choices.text: The large skin test confirms peanut allergy
This is likely coincidental and peanut can be reintroduced
The clinical history of immediate reaction plus positive skin test strongly supports IgE-mediated
An oral food challenge is required before diagnosis

explanation:
Correct Answer: Correct! The combination of convincing history (immediate symptoms after ingestion) plus positive testing = high probability of true allergy. Oral food challenge could confirm but is often not needed with clear history.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Skin test shows sensitization, not clinical allergy. However, combined with convincing history, diagnosis is likely.
- With clear history and positive testing, OFC is not required and would be risky.
- Immediate reaction with hives and vomiting after peanut exposure is highly consistent with IgE-mediated allergy.

Evidence: NIAID Food Allergy Guidelines 2010/Addendum 2017 - Togias A, et al. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2017;139(1):29-44

Why other answers are incorrect:
- The large skin test confirms peanut allergy: Skin test shows sensitization, not clinical allergy. However, combined with convincing history, diagnosis is likely.
- An oral food challenge is required before diagnosis: With clear history and positive testing, OFC is not required and would be risky.
- This is likely coincidental and peanut can be reintroduced: Immediate reaction with hives and vomiting after peanut exposure is highly consistent with IgE-mediated allergy.

Reference: NIAID Food Allergy Guidelines 2010/Addendum 2017 - https://www.jacionline.org/ [Accessed 2025]
Reference: Allergy-immunology - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/ [Accessed 2025]

explanation:

- Would show fungi on biopsy, not eosinophils.
- Different mechanism - would show aperistalsis.

- Candida esophagitis: Would show fungi on biopsy, not eosinophils.
- Achalasia: Different mechanism - would show aperistalsis.

explanation:
Correct Answer: Correct! C4 is low in HAE even BETWEEN attacks. Best screening test.

- HAE is not IgE-mediated.
- For mast cell disorders, not HAE.
- Won't work - HAE is bradykinin-mediated.

stem: A 42-year-old female presents to the clinic, stating: "I have this itchy rash on my hands that won't go away. It's been months and nothing helps."Vital SignsBP 120/78HR 72RR 14Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 99% Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Irritant Contact Dermatitis is the most likely diagnosis. Wet work and chemical exposure can cause irritant dermatitis - may coexist.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Treating with steroids indefinitely without identifying cause
- Not considering occupational exposures
- Missing common allergens (fragrance, preservatives in 'hypoallergenic' products)
- Not doing comprehensive patch testing

Evidence: ACDS Contact Dermatitis Guidelines - American Contact Dermatitis Society Guidelines

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Atopic Dermatitis (Hand Eczema): No childhood eczema history. Adult-onset hand eczema is usually contact-related.
- Psoriasis: Can affect hands but usually has other features (nail pitting, plaques elsewhere).

Reference: ACDS Contact Dermatitis Guidelines - https://www.contactderm.org/ [Accessed 2025]
Reference: Allergy-immunology - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 35-year-old male presents to the emergency department, stating: "I have a rash all over my body and high fever. I started a new seizure medication about 3 weeks ago."Vital SignsBP 108/68HR 112RR 18Temp 103.2°FSpO₂ 96% Patient started phenytoin 3 weeks ago, now has fever, diffuse rash, facial swelling, and eosinophilia. What is the MOST important immediate action?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Correct! Stopping the culprit drug is the most critical intervention. DRESS has 10% mortality, mainly from liver failure.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- WRONG - must stop the culprit drug immediately.
- Carbamazepine has high cross-reactivity with phenytoin - contraindicated.
- Delay is dangerous - stop drug immediately.

Evidence: RegiSCAR DRESS Diagnostic Criteria - Kardaun SH, et al. Br J Dermatol. 2013;169(5):1071-1080

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Continue phenytoin and add antihistamines: WRONG - must stop the culprit drug immediately.
- Switch to carbamazepine: Carbamazepine has high cross-reactivity with phenytoin - contraindicated.
- Observe for 48 hours: Delay is dangerous - stop drug immediately.

stem: A 16-year-old boy presents with 2 years of nasal congestion, clear rhinorrhea, sneezing, and itchy nose/eyes that worsen in spring and fall. He has dark discoloration under both eyes. Nasal mucosa appears pale, boggy, and edematous with clear secretions. He also has a transverse nasal crease from repeatedly pushing his nose upward (the "allergic salute").ExamEyes Bilateral conjunctival injection, Dennie-Morgan lines (infraorbital folds)Nose Pale boggy turbinates, clear rhinorrhea, transverse nasal creaseThroat Cobblestoning of posterior pharynx (lymphoid hyperplasia)Skin Dry patches in antecubital fossae (mild eczema) What is the diagnosis, and what physical exam findings are specific to this condition?

choices.text: Allergic rhinitis — diagnosed clinically by the constellation of:
Vasomotor rhinitis — nonallergic rhinitis triggered by irritants

explanation:
Correct Answer: Allergic rhinitis: IgE-mediated type I hypersensitivity reaction of the nasal mucosa. 10-30% of adults, 40% of children. Physical exam findings that DISTINGUISH allergic from non-allergic rhinitis: pale boggy turbinates (infection = erythematous), allergic shiners, Dennie-Morgan lines, nasal crease, cobblestoning. Atopic triad: allergic rhinitis + asthma + atopic dermatitis (atopic march in children: eczema → food allergy → allergic rhinitis → asthma). Treatment hierarchy: (1) Allergen avoidance, (2) Intranasal corticosteroids (FIRST-LINE — most effective single therapy for all symptoms), (3) Oral antihistamines (best for sneezing/pruritus/rhinorrhea, less effective for congestion), (4) Leukotriene antagonist (montelukast, second-line), (5) Allergen immunotherapy (subcutaneous SCIT or sublingual SLIT for refractory cases). Classification: intermittent vs persistent, mild vs moderate-severe.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 2-year history with seasonal worsening = chronic allergic pattern (not acute infection)
- Clear rhinorrhea (not purulent) = allergic (infection = yellow/green)
- Pale boggy turbinates = allergic (infection = red/inflamed)
- Allergic shiners + Dennie-Morgan lines + nasal crease = classic allergic phenotype
- Spring and fall worsening = pollen seasons (tree in spring, ragweed in fall)
- Eczema in antecubital fossae = atopic dermatitis (atopic triad)
- Cobblestoning = chronic postnasal drip stimulating lymphoid tissue

Evidence: Allergic Rhinitis - StatPearls - Dykewicz MS, et al. StatPearls. NBK538186. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Common cold: The common cold is a viral infection lasting 7-10 days, not 2 YEARS. Colds produce purulent discharge (yellow/green), erythematous nasal mucosa, and do NOT cause allergic shiners, Dennie-Morgan lines, or nasal crease. The seasonal pattern with spring/fall worsening corresponds to pollen seasons, not viral epidemics.
- Vasomotor rhinitis: Vasomotor (nonallergic) rhinitis is triggered by irritants (cold air, strong odors, humidity changes) and presents with congestion and rhinorrhea WITHOUT itching or sneezing. Nasal mucosa is erythematous (not pale). There are no allergic shiners, Dennie-Morgan lines, or eczema. The prominent pruritus and classic allergic exam findings distinguish allergic rhinitis.
- Chronic sinusitis: Chronic sinusitis (>12 weeks) presents with facial pain/pressure, purulent nasal discharge, postnasal drip, and hyposmia. CT sinus shows mucosal thickening or air-fluid levels. This patient has CLEAR rhinorrhea, pale boggy turbinates (not purulent congestion), and characteristic allergic exam findings — all pointing to allergic rhinitis, not sinusitis.
- Nasal polyps: Nasal polyps can cause bilateral obstruction and are associated with chronic rhinosinusitis and aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (Samter's triad). On exam, polyps appear as pale glistening masses in the nasal cavity. This patient's exam shows pale boggy TURBINATES (normal structures that are swollen) with classic allergic findings, not polyps.

Reference: Allergic Rhinitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK538186/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with intensely pruritic raised wheals on her trunk and arms that appeared 30 minutes after eating shrimp for the first time in 6 months. The lesions are erythematous, well-circumscribed, blanchable, and range from 1-5 cm. Individual wheals resolve within 12 hours but new ones keep appearing. She has no angioedema, respiratory symptoms, or hemodynamic changes.Vital SignsBP 118/72HR 78SpO2 99%ExamSkin Multiple erythematous well-circumscribed wheals 1-5cm, trunk and arms, blanchable, intensely pruriticOropharynx No lip/tongue swellingLungs ClearCardiovascular Stable What is the diagnosis, and how do you distinguish this from anaphylaxis?

choices.text: Acute urticaria (hives) WITHOUT anaphylaxis — IgE-mediated mast cell

explanation:
Correct Answer: Urticaria vs anaphylaxis: the critical distinction. Urticaria alone: skin-only IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation → dermal edema (wheals) + pruritus. Treatment: antihistamines. Anaphylaxis: SYSTEMIC mast cell degranulation involving ≥2 organ systems (skin + respiratory + GI + cardiovascular). Treatment: EPINEPHRINE IM (thigh). Anaphylaxis criteria (any 1 of 3): (1) Acute skin symptoms + respiratory compromise or hypotension, (2) ≥2 systems after exposure to likely allergen: skin, respiratory, GI (vomiting, cramping), cardiovascular, (3) Hypotension after exposure to KNOWN allergen. Urticaria features: wheals are TRANSIENT (individual lesion 24h and is PAINFUL (not pruritic) → consider urticarial vasculitis (biopsy shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis). Acute: Chronic: >6 weeks (most chronic urticaria is autoimmune, not allergic).

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Erythematous wheals appearing 30 min after allergen exposure = IgE-mediated urticaria
- Individual lesions resolve <12 hours = classic transient wheals
- Blanchable = dermal edema (not purpura/vasculitis)
- Intensely pruritic = histamine-mediated (not painful = not vasculitis)
- NO respiratory symptoms, NO GI symptoms, NO hypotension = NOT anaphylaxis
- Hemodynamically stable with normal SpO2 = skin-only involvement
- Shrimp = common food allergen (shellfish tropomyosin is the allergenic protein)

Evidence: Urticaria - StatPearls - Zuberbier T, et al. StatPearls. NBK538194. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Anaphylaxis requiring epi: Anaphylaxis requires involvement of ≥2 organ systems or hypotension after known allergen exposure. This patient has skin involvement ONLY (urticaria) with NO respiratory symptoms (clear lungs, SpO2 99%), NO GI symptoms, and stable hemodynamics (BP 118/72). Isolated urticaria, even if from an allergen, does NOT constitute anaphylaxis. However, the patient should be monitored and counseled about anaphylaxis signs.
- Contact dermatitis: Contact dermatitis is a type IV delayed hypersensitivity reaction (not IgE-mediated) that occurs 24-72 hours after skin contact with an allergen. It presents as eczematous patches (vesicles, erythema, scaling) confined to the area of contact, NOT as migratory transient wheals. This patient's reaction was SYSTEMIC (trunk and arms) from ingested shellfish, not from skin contact.
- Erythema multiforme: Erythema multiforme (EM) produces TARGET LESIONS (concentric rings of color change) that are FIXED (remain at the same site for days-weeks), often on palms/soles. EM is most commonly triggered by HSV infection, not food. Urticaria produces TRANSIENT wheals that migrate and resolve within 24 hours — a completely different morphology.
- Mastocytosis: Systemic mastocytosis involves abnormal mast cell proliferation with chronic/recurrent urticaria, flushing, and GI symptoms. It is diagnosed by elevated serum tryptase (>20 ng/mL), skin biopsy showing mast cell clusters, or bone marrow biopsy with KIT D816V mutation. This patient's acute episode triggered by a specific food allergen is far more consistent with IgE-mediated acute urticaria than systemic mast cell disease.

Reference: Urticaria - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK538194/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 3-year-old boy has had 8 ear infections, 2 episodes of bacterial pneumonia, and 1 episode of bacterial sinusitis in the past 18 months. Each episode required antibiotics and resolved slowly. He also had persistent diarrhea from Giardia lamblia. His growth is at the 10th percentile (was 50th at birth). Family history: maternal uncle died of pneumonia at age 4.LabsCBC WBC 9,200 (normal differential, adequate lymphocytes)IgG 85 mg/dL (normal 345-1,236)IgA IgM 22 mg/dL (normal 43-207)IgE NormalFlow cytometry B cells What diagnosis explains the pattern, and what is the inheritance?

choices.text: X-linked agammaglobulinemia (XLA, Bruton disease) — caused by a mutation in BRUTON
Selective IgA deficiency — most common primary immunodeficiency

explanation:
Correct Answer: When to suspect primary immunodeficiency (WARNING SIGNS): ≥4 ear infections/year, ≥2 serious sinus infections/year, ≥2 months on antibiotics with little effect, ≥2 pneumonias/year, failure to thrive, recurrent deep abscesses, persistent thrush or skin infections, need for IV antibiotics, family history of immunodeficiency, 2+ deep-seated infections. This patient meets MULTIPLE criteria. XLA (Bruton): ABSENT B cells + panhypogammaglobulinemia + X-linked. Key discriminators from other antibody deficiencies: (1) B cells BTK pathway: BTK is required for pre-B to mature B cell transition in bone marrow. Without BTK, no mature B cells → no immunoglobulin production. IVIG: passive immunoglobulin replacement every 3-4 weeks; transforms prognosis from fatal in childhood to near-normal lifespan.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 8 ear infections + 2 pneumonias + sinusitis in 18 months = far exceeds normal frequency
- Giardia = classic pathogen in antibody deficiency (IgA protects GI mucosa)
- IgG 85, IgA <7, IgM 22 = panhypogammaglobulinemia (all classes profoundly low)
- B cells <1% on flow cytometry = absence of mature B cells (BTK mutation blocks maturation)
- T cells normal = NOT SCID (which has T cell deficiency)
- Male patient + maternal uncle died young = X-linked inheritance pattern
- Onset after 6 months = timing consistent with waning maternal IgG

Evidence: X-linked Agammaglobulinemia - StatPearls - Conley ME, et al. StatPearls. NBK539814. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Normal childhood infections: Normal children have 6-8 URI per year, but these are mild viral infections. This child has 8 ear infections + 2 bacterial pneumonias + bacterial sinusitis requiring prolonged antibiotics + Giardia + failure to thrive — far exceeding normal frequency and severity. The Jeffrey Modell Foundation warning signs suggest that ≥4 ear infections, ≥2 pneumonias, or ≥2 months of antibiotics without effect should trigger immunodeficiency workup.
- Selective IgA deficiency: Selective IgA deficiency (most common primary immunodeficiency, 1:500) has LOW IgA with NORMAL IgG and IgM. This patient has profoundly low IgG (85), IgA (<7), AND IgM (22) — panhypogammaglobulinemia. Also, selective IgA deficiency has NORMAL B cell numbers, while this patient has B cells <1%.
- CVID: CVID also causes hypogammaglobulinemia with recurrent infections, but: (1) CVID has B cells PRESENT (normal or reduced numbers, NOT absent), (2) CVID typically presents in the 2nd-3rd decade (not age 3), (3) CVID has variable inheritance (not X-linked). This patient's absent B cells (<1%) and X-linked family history (maternal uncle) point to XLA, not CVID.
- SCID: SCID presents EARLIER (first weeks-months of life) with MORE SEVERE infections including opportunistic organisms (PJP, CMV, Candida). SCID involves T CELL deficiency (which secondarily impairs B cell function). This patient has NORMAL T cells on flow cytometry, excluding SCID. Also, SCID patients typically present before 6 months with failure to thrive and lymphopenia.

Reference: XLA - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK539814/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 7-year-old boy develops diffuse urticaria, lip and tongue swelling, and wheezing within 10 minutes of eating a cookie at a birthday party. His mother reports he has a known tree nut allergy. He is now speaking in short phrases and pulling at his shirt. Vital Signs BP 80/50 HR 148 RR 32 SpO₂ 90% What is the single most important immediate intervention?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Epinephrine IM in the anterolateral thigh is the FIRST and ONLY life-saving treatment for anaphylaxis. There is no contraindication to epinephrine in anaphylaxis. Delay in epinephrine administration is the primary risk factor for fatal anaphylaxis. Pediatric dose: 0.01 mg/kg of 1:1000 (max 0.3 mg in prepubertal, 0.5 mg in adolescents). Antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts, NOT substitutes.

Evidence: Anaphylaxis Pediatric Food - StatPearls - Reber LL, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK482124. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Acute urticaria: Urticaria alone without respiratory or cardiovascular involvement is not anaphylaxis.
- Foreign body aspiration: Sudden stridor in toddler but no urticaria, no exposure history, no systemic symptoms.
- Viral croup: Barking cough, stridor, but gradual onset, no urticaria, no hypotension.
- Hereditary angioedema: No urticaria, no hypotension. Bradykinin-mediated, not IgE-mediated. Does not respond to epinephrine.

Reference: Anaphylaxis Pediatric Food - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482124/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 72-year-old woman on metoprolol for atrial fibrillation receives IV cefazolin prior to hip replacement surgery. Within 5 minutes, she develops generalized flushing, diffuse urticaria, and reports "throat tightness." Her blood pressure drops despite IV fluids. The anesthesiologist notes bronchospasm on auscultation. Vital Signs BP 72/40 HR 62 (on metoprolol) SpO₂ 86% What unique challenge does this patient's beta-blocker therapy present in anaphylaxis management? Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.text: Epinephrine is contraindicated in patients on beta-blockers
Beta-blockers are protective and reduce anaphylaxis severity
Beta-blockers may blunt the response to epinephrine, requiring higher doses or glucagon as adjunct
Switch from epinephrine to phenylephrine as first-line
Administer IV calcium instead of epinephrine

explanation:
Correct Answer: Beta-blockers complicate anaphylaxis in two ways: (1) blunt compensatory tachycardia (HR may be low/normal despite severe shock), and (2) reduce effectiveness of epinephrine (beta-receptor blockade impairs bronchodilation and cardiac stimulation). Epinephrine is STILL first-line — there is NEVER a contraindication in anaphylaxis. If refractory, add glucagon 1-5 mg IV (bypasses beta-receptor, activates adenylate cyclase directly). May need higher/repeated epinephrine doses.

Evidence: Anaphylaxis Elderly Medication - StatPearls - Reber LL, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK482124. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Vasovagal syncope: Bradycardia + hypotension but no urticaria, no bronchospasm. Resolves with supine position.
- Acute MI: Chest pain, ST changes but no urticaria or bronchospasm. Can coexist (Kounis syndrome).
- Cardiogenic shock: Hypotension with JVD and crackles but no urticaria/angioedema.
- Sepsis: Hypotension with fever but onset over hours, not minutes after drug exposure.

Reference: Anaphylaxis Elderly Medication - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482124/ [Accessed 2025]
auditNote: Not yet verified — requires source-level audit before student-facing use

stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with a 4-month history of daily hives that appear spontaneously and resolve within 24 hours without scarring. She has tried cetirizine 20 mg daily (updosed) for 4 weeks without relief. Review of systems is negative for fevers, arthralgias, and weight loss.Vital SignsBP 118/72HR 76RR 14Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 99% CBC, CMP, TSH, and CRP are normal. ANA is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.text: Start omalizumab 300 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks
Start prednisone 40 mg daily with 2-week taper
Obtain skin biopsy to rule out urticarial vasculitis
Switch to hydroxyzine 25 mg three times daily

explanation:
Correct Answer: For chronic spontaneous urticaria refractory to updosed second-generation antihistamines, omalizumab (anti-IgE) is the recommended next-line therapy per international guidelines.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- CSU requires hives >6 weeks without identifiable trigger
- Step-up: standard antihistamine → updosed (4x) → omalizumab → cyclosporine
- Systemic corticosteroids limited to short courses for acute flares only
- Montelukast has weak evidence in CSU and is not a primary add-on

Evidence: Chronic Urticaria - StatPearls - Antia C, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK555910. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Add montelukast: Weak evidence in CSU; not recommended as standard step-up before omalizumab.
- Start prednisone taper: Steroids are for acute flares, not maintenance. Chronic use causes significant adverse effects.
- Skin biopsy: Indicated only if individual lesions last >24 hours, are painful, or leave purpura suggestive of vasculitis.
- Switch to hydroxyzine: First-generation antihistamines cause sedation and anticholinergic effects; not preferred over omalizumab step-up.

Reference: Chronic Urticaria - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK555910/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 52-year-old woman with ovarian cancer requires carboplatin-based chemotherapy. During her 7th cycle, she developed diffuse urticaria, throat tightness, and hypotension within 10 minutes of infusion start, consistent with a carboplatin hypersensitivity reaction. She was treated with epinephrine and recovered. Skin testing to carboplatin is positive.Vital SignsBP 122/78HR 82RR 16Temp 98.6°FSpO₂ 98% No alternative chemotherapy agents are available for her cancer. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Rechallenge with carboplatin at a slower infusion rate
Perform rapid drug desensitization to carboplatin
Premedicate with diphenhydramine and dexamethasone, then re-infuse at standard rate
Switch to cisplatin as a platinum alternative

explanation:
Correct Answer: Rapid drug desensitization allows safe re-administration of essential medications in patients with confirmed IgE-mediated hypersensitivity when no alternative exists. Performed in ICU settings with incrementally increasing doses.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Desensitization temporarily induces tolerance by gradually exposing mast cells to increasing drug concentrations
- Must be repeated for each subsequent dose — tolerance is not permanent
- Performed under close monitoring, typically in ICU with 12-16 step protocols
- Cross-reactivity between carboplatin and cisplatin is high (~50%), making cisplatin switch unsafe

Evidence: Drug Desensitization - StatPearls - Cernadas JR, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK563296. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Rechallenge at slower rate: Slowing infusion alone does not prevent IgE-mediated reactions; desensitization protocol is required.
- Permanently discontinue: Incorrect when no alternative exists and desensitization is available and effective.
- Premedicate and re-infuse: Premedication alone does not prevent anaphylaxis in confirmed IgE-mediated reactions.
- Switch to cisplatin: High cross-reactivity (~50%) between platinum agents; switch is not reliably safe.

Reference: Drug Desensitization - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK563296/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 28-year-old man has a history of recurrent sinopulmonary infections since childhood. He was diagnosed with common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) 2 years ago and started on subcutaneous immunoglobulin (SCIG) replacement. He now presents with his third pneumonia in 6 months despite adherent SCIG therapy. His current IgG trough level is 420 mg/dL (goal >500).Vital SignsBP 130/82HR 88RR 20Temp 101.2°FSpO₂ 95% After treating the acute pneumonia, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in his immunoglobulin management?

choices.text: Continue current SCIG dose and monitor
Increase SCIG dose to achieve trough IgG >700 mg/dL
Switch from SCIG to IVIG for better efficacy
Add prophylactic azithromycin and discontinue SCIG
Obtain CT chest and discontinue immunoglobulin if bronchiectasis found

explanation:
Correct Answer: In CVID patients with breakthrough infections despite immunoglobulin replacement, the dose should be increased to achieve higher trough IgG levels (≥700–800 mg/dL). Clinical response, not just trough level, guides dosing.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Goal IgG trough is individualized — some patients need >700-800 mg/dL to prevent infections
- SCIG and IVIG have equivalent efficacy when dosed appropriately
- Prophylactic antibiotics may be adjunctive but do not replace immunoglobulin
- Bronchiectasis is a complication of recurrent infections, not a reason to stop Ig replacement

Evidence: Common Variable Immunodeficiency - StatPearls - Tam JS, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK499866. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Continue current dose: Trough is below target and patient is having breakthrough infections — dose increase is needed.
- Switch SCIG to IVIG: Equivalent efficacy; the issue is inadequate dosing, not the route of administration.
- Discontinue SCIG and add azithromycin: Prophylactic antibiotics complement but do not replace immunoglobulin in CVID.
- Discontinue if bronchiectasis found: Bronchiectasis is a consequence of recurrent infections and an indication to continue, not stop, Ig replacement.

Reference: CVID - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK499866/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 22-year-old woman with severe atopic dermatitis covering 40% BSA presents for follow-up. She has failed topical corticosteroids (clobetasol), tacrolimus, and a 3-month course of cyclosporine (discontinued due to rising creatinine). Her EASI score is 28 (severe). She reports significant sleep disturbance and depression related to her skin disease.Vital SignsBP 116/68HR 72RR 14Temp 98.2°FSpO₂ 99% Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

choices.text: Restart cyclosporine at a lower dose with renal monitoring
Start dupilumab 600 mg loading then 300 mg every 2 weeks
Refer for phototherapy (narrowband UVB) three times weekly
Start mycophenolate mofetil 1 g twice daily

explanation:
Correct Answer: Dupilumab (anti-IL-4Rα, blocking IL-4 and IL-13) is first-line biologic therapy for moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis refractory to topical therapy. It has superior efficacy and safety profile compared to systemic immunosuppressants.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Dupilumab is indicated for moderate-severe AD in patients ≥6 months who failed topical therapy
- SOLO 1 & 2 trials: 36-38% achieved EASI-75 vs 10% placebo
- Most common side effect: conjunctivitis (injection-site reactions less common)
- Cyclosporine caused nephrotoxicity — restarting is inappropriate; methotrexate/MMF are second-line systemics

Evidence: Atopic Dermatitis - StatPearls - Kolb L, Ferrer-Bruker SJ. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK448071. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Restart cyclosporine: Previously caused nephrotoxicity; restarting at any dose carries continued renal risk.
- Start methotrexate: Has moderate evidence in AD but less effective and more toxicity than dupilumab; second-line option.
- Phototherapy: Effective but requires 3x/week clinic visits; impractical for many patients and less effective than dupilumab for severe AD.
- Start mycophenolate: Off-label for AD with limited evidence; requires monitoring and has GI/hematologic side effects.

Reference: Atopic Dermatitis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK448071/ [Accessed 2025]
